name: MAGGIE O'FARRELL
description:
Event 2
Maggie O'Farrell

We don’t play favourites with our Festival writers. Honest. Yet every so often, a writer seems to be having a particular moment in the international book world – and it’s safe to say that Maggie O’Farrell is that writer, having that moment.

Last year marked O’Farrell’s 25th year of publishing, with celebrations honouring a quarter century of books including After You’d Gone, The Hand That First Held Mine, This Must Be the Place, I Am, I Am, I Am, Hamnet and The Marriage Portrait.

Meanwhile, the recent film adaptation of Hamnet directed by Chloé Zhao has earned eight Oscar nominations, including one for O’Farrell for the screenplay. And in early June, her major new novel Land will reach eager readers.

In this special conversation to help open AWF26, O’Farrell speaks to Michelle Langstone live from her home in Edinburgh about her extraordinary year, and all that led to it.

name: PATRICK RADDEN KEEFE
description:
Event 3
Patrick Radden Keefe

When Patrick Radden Keefe was awarded the Baillie Gifford Prize for Empire of Pain, his exposé of The Sackler Family’s role in the opioid crisis, the judges praised its “moral rigour, its controlled fury, its exhaustive research, the bravery it took to write it … [and] its sheer propulsive narrative energy”. These qualities lie at the heart of Keefe’s oeuvre.

Trained as a lawyer at Yale and one of The New Yorker’s most decorated long-time staff writers, Keefe’s blend of scrupulous reporting, literary skill and deep commitment to truth and justice has helped bring untold human stories to light, systems of power to account, and prompted shifts in public understanding and policy debates.

From Say Nothing, his Orwell Prize for Political Writing-winning account of a civilian killing during The Troubles, to Chatter (dissecting global eavesdropping), The Snakehead (exposing illegal Chinese smuggling into the US), Empire of Pain and his newest work London Falling (investigating a teenager’s tragic end in the circles of the super-rich), it’s a remarkable body of work.

Jack Tame meets him in his much-anticipated first AWF appearance.

Supported by Platinum Patron Anna Gibbons.

name: LUNCH WITH PATRICK RADDEN KEEFE
description:
Event 4
Lunch with Patrick Radden Keefe

A rare chance to be part of an intimate lunch with one of The New Yorker’s most esteemed staff writers and the Orwell and Baillie Gifford Prize-winning author of Say Nothing and Empire of Pain, Patrick Radden Keefe.

He sits down with Simon Wilson to reflect on his illustrious career at the forefront of investigative journalism and introduce his new book London Falling, about the mysterious 2019 death of a London teenager, and his family’s search to understand the secret second life he was leading beneath the city’s glittering surface.

Over a delicious two-course lunch he’ll share insights from his life’s work, reveal what the years of rigorous reporting behind his books entail, and take some of your questions.

name: OCKHAM NEW ZEALAND BOOK AWARDS 2026
description:
Event 5
Ockham New Zealand Book Awards 2026

Once a year, the creators of the nation’s finest books step into the spotlight. The prestigious Ockham New Zealand Book Awards bring together writers, publishers, booksellers and readers for a celebration of stories, ideas and craft on the big stage.

First up is the presentation of the Mātātuhi Foundation Best First Book awards. Then, guided by MC Miriama Kamo (Ngāi Tahu, Ngāti Mutunga), the judges will announce the winners of the General Non-Fiction Award, the Mary and Peter Biggs Award for Poetry, the BookHub Award for Illustrated Non-Fiction, and finally the $65,000 Jann Medlicott Acorn Prize for Fiction. You will also hear from all the finalists. This is the night to discover, applaud and be immersed in New Zealand’s best writing. All welcome!

Finalists

General Non-Fiction Award: Jacinda Ardern; Naomi Arnold; Peta Carey; Tina Makereti (Te Ātiawa, Ngāti Tūwharetoa, Ngāti Rangatahi-Matakore, Pākehā)

Mary and Peter Biggs Award for Poetry: Nafanua Purcell Kersel (Satupa‘itea, Faleālupo, Aleipata, Tuaefu); Sophie van Waardenberg; Erik Kennedy; Anna Jackson

BookHub Award for Illustrated Non-Fiction: Charlotte Macdonald; Philip Garnock-Jones; Sarah Farrar; Elizabeth Cox

Jann Medlicott Acorn Prize for Fiction: Ingrid Horrocks; Laura Vincent (Ngāti Māhanga, Ngāpuhi); Sam Mahon; Catherine Chidgey

Note: This session can be included in Ticket Bundles selections.

name: FESTIVAL GALA NIGHT: RESETTING THE COMPASS
description:
Event 6
Festival Gala Night: Resetting the Compass

Life takes us in many directions, but not always the right ones. In the Festival’s major opening night, eight exceptional writers speak to a moment in their life or work where their compass was reset – by choice or by chance; hard-won or by happenstance; early in life or in its later acts – that set them on a course truer to themselves.

Lining up to tell us about their new Norths, their True Norths, and the moments of reckoning, rebellion, resilience, reinvention, resolution or raw nerve it took to find them are:

One of Aotearoa’s most distinguished novelists Witi Ihimaera (Te Whānau a Kai, Te Aitanga a Māhaki, Rongowhakaata, Ngāti Ira and Ngāti Porou); acclaimed artist and activist Tāme Iti (Ngāi Tūhoe); grief expert and best-selling author Dr Lucy Hone; Women’s Prize for Fiction winner and New York Times best-selling author Tayari Jones; Prime Minister’s Award for Literary Achievement-winning author of 14 novels Elizabeth Knox; #1 New York Times and Sunday Times best-selling author RF Kuang; Booker Prize-winning Canadian novelist Yann Martel; Edinburgh’s Poet Laureate and prize-winning Scottish author Michael Pedersen.

Hosted by Miriama McDowell (Ngāti Hine, Ngāpuhi).

name: SĀMOAN MADE SIMPLE: ALPHABET AND PRONUNCIATION
description:
Event 7
Sāmoan Made Simple: Alphabet and Pronunciation

Start your day with language teacher Jason Tiatia as he guides you through some of the basics of the Sāmoan language with fun, buildable exercises for all levels.

name: INDUSTRY INSIGHTS WITH THE BIG IDEA: AUTHOR EDITION
description:
Event 8
Industry Insights with The Big Idea: Author Edition

Bite-sized interviews between The Big Idea Editor Gabi Lardies and practitioners and professionals in the NZ literary sector. Today she talks with AWF featured writers about their road to getting published.

name: CREATIVE RIGHTS IN THE AGE OF AI
description:
Event 9
Creative Rights in the Age of AI

Anxious about AI? Copyright Licensing New Zealand is here to guide writers – or interested readers – navigating the rapidly changing intersections of creativity, copyright and AI technology.

Presentation followed by Q+A.

name: DEBUT OF THE DAY: LAURA VINCENT
description:
Event 10
Debut of the Day: Laura Vincent

Laura Vincent (Ngāti Māhanga, Ngāpuhi) will introduce you to the matriarchs of the Gordon whānau as she reads from her Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted debut novel Hoods Landing.

name: WORLDWIDE BOOK CLUB: PASIFIKA LITERATURE
description:
Event 11
Worldwide Book Club: Pasifika Literature

Join Inangaro Vakaafi, of Niue (Mutalau) and Cook Islands (Manihiki, Atiu) heritage, as she shares Pasifika writers and books she’d love you to know about.

name: DANIEL HAHN: AN INTRODUCTION TO TRANSLATION
description:
Event 12
Daniel Hahn: An Introduction to Translation

Daniel Hahn is one of the UK’s most acclaimed literary translators, known for his translations from Portuguese, Spanish and French and his work with the Translators Association, the Society of Authors and English PEN.

In this illuminating and beginner-friendly masterclass, he’ll reveal how a piece of fiction moves between languages in the hands of a literary translator.

Together you’ll work through small exercises accompanied by guided discussion of the choices, challenges, quirks and joys that translators encounter when working on a text, and have a go at some small bits of translation of your own.

Suitable for everyone, regardless of whether you know another language. Only curiosity (and tolerance for some language geekery) is required.

name: INSIDE THE STORY: THE ART AND THE ETHICS OF INVESTIGATIVE JOURNALISM
description:
Event 13
Inside the Story: The Art and the Ethics of Investigative Journalism

Whether covering The Troubles, the opioid epidemic, or London’s underworld, Patrick Radden Keefe is renowned for illuminating human stories in his scrupulous interrogation of power systems. Barbara Demick is unparalleled in her meticulous reporting on authoritarian regimes (North Korea, Tibet, China) and capturing the lives of those within. Closer to home, next-generation talent Asher Emanuel’s The Valley is a landmark examination of New Zealand’s criminal justice system and two young men enmeshed in it.

Fascinating and vital systems to interrogate but all with human lives – often vulnerable ones – at their centre. How close is too close for a writer to get? And where is the line between capturing the right story and doing the right thing?

Adam Dudding meets them to discuss.

name: THE JANN MEDLICOTT ACORN PRIZE FOR FICTION 2026 WINNER
description:
Event 14
The Jann Medlicott Acorn Prize for Fiction 2026 Winner

Be among the first to hear from the newly announced winner of the country’s biggest literary award in their first public interview since winning the career-changing prize. With the winning author revealed at the Ockham NZ Book Awards during Festival week, we can’t tell you yet who will be in the interviewee seat.

However, with recent recipients of the calibre of Damien Wilkins, Catherine Chidgey, Emily Perkins and Becky Manawatu, you’re going to want a front-row seat.

The winner joins convenor of the fiction judges Craig Cliff to discuss their winning book and writing journey.

name: THE COST OF CARE
description:
Event 15
The Cost of Care

Best-selling medical memoirs The Unlikely Doctor by Dr Timoti Te Moke and Everything but the Medicine by Lucy O’Hagan ask urgent questions about what good care in contemporary Aotearoa New Zealand means – and about who gets to give it.

Te Moke’s remarkable story moves from gang life and prison to becoming a doctor at the age of 56. O’Hagan’s spans medical school misogyny, fighting for those on the margins, and learning self-care after burning out helping others.

They join Dale Husband (Ngāti Maru) for a powerful conversation about what being a good doctor in our country today means; their varied routes to and through the profession; and what barriers, boundaries and biases must be dismantled if we are to care for those who need it most.

name: ALEXANDRA LAPIERRE
description:
Event 16
Alexandra Lapierre

Alexandra Lapierre is a best-selling French novelist, biographer and short story writer. Educated at the Sorbonne and in the US, she has been awarded some of France’s highest literary honours and earned recognition from readers and critics worldwide.

Her writing is driven by a desire to bring captivating figures and overlooked moments of history to vivid life. Her biography Fanny Stevenson (about the life of Robert Louis Stevenson’s wife) won the Grand Prix des Lectrices de ELLE and her historical novel Artemisia (about the Baroque painter Artemisia Gentileschi) became an international bestseller.

Her most recent work, The Very Secretive and Passionate Stella Miles Franklin, focuses on rural Australia’s bold young woman who battled misogyny in the literary world.

Caroline Barron (Te Uri O Hau, Pākehā) meets her.

Supported by the Embassy of France.

name: GARRISON WORLD: REDCOAT SOLDIERS IN AOTEAROA NEW ZEALAND
description:
Event 17
Garrison World: Redcoat Soldiers in Aotearoa New Zealand

From the 1840s until 1870, thousands of British soldiers lived and served here. They built roads, patrolled garrison towns and fought in wars. Their presence – and the violence it often entailed – was central to the transformation of a Māori world into a settler colony.

In this illuminating lecture, distinguished Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted historian Charlotte Macdonald draws on a lifetime of scholarship, rich archival data and personal accounts to explore the lives of men (and some women) stationed here and examines the structures, experiences and lasting legacy of military occupation on the country.

Looking beyond our shores, Macdonald also demonstrates how events in Aotearoa were linked to rebellion in India and uprising in Jamaica, as imperial power was tested and asserted across the 19th century.

name: SIOBHAN HARVEY: MASTERING PLACE AND SPACE
description:
Event 18
Siobhan Harvey: Mastering Place and Space

All story happens somewhere. Be it fiction, poetry, memoir, creative nonfiction, sci-fi, fantasy, hybrid form or any of the diverse genres in which we write, setting is vital to the success of our work.

To coincide with the release of her award-winning memoir, What We Remember, What We Forget, join queer, migrant author Siobhan Harvey for a masterclass on crafting the place and space in narratives. Along the way, you’ll consider the roles of mimesis, diegesis, pathetic fallacy and character interiority in helping evoke literary landscapes as more than just stages upon which plots are enacted.

This is an immersive masterclass, so bring your stories and creativity, and be ready to ponder, reflect, write and edit.

name: GONE BY LUNCHTIME: SIX MONTHS TO THE POLLS
description:
Event 19
Gone by Lunchtime: Six Months to the Polls

NZ’s favourite politics podcast is back with a special live version for AWF26. And with the country six months from a national election that’s likely to go to the wire, they won’t be short of things to talk about.

Bringing their customary mix of insight, analysis and humour to our headline stage are Toby Manhire of The Spinoff, executive producer of Mata Reports Annabelle Lee-Mather (Ngāi Tahu, Ngāti Kahungunu, Ngāti Māmoe) and former press secretary in the Key government Ben Thomas. Together they go beyond the Beehive’s doors to offer their predictions on who will take the top seat(s) and what the road to election day will hold for the country, and for those vying to lead it.

name: MEET THE CANADIAN WRITERS
description:
Event 20
Meet the Canadian Writers

We are delighted to host a stellar line-up of writers from Canada at the Festival this year.

Spanning Vancouver to Saskatchewan, Ottawa to Winnipeg, as well as fiction, non-fiction and writing for young people, are:

Esteemed Booker Prize-winning novelist of Life of Pi Yann Martel (Son of Nobody)

Leading political commentator and writer David Moscrop (Too Dumb for Democracy?, Elbows Up!, On Nationalism)

2025 Booker Prize-longlisted Ukrainian-Canadian author of Endling and Good Citizens Need not Fear Maria Reva

Double Governor General’s Literary Award-winning writer for children and adults and member of Norway House Cree Nation David A. Robertson (The Misewa Saga, 52 Ways to Reconcile, All the Little Monsters)

They join Artistic Director Lyndsey Fineran for introductory interviews about their work, share insights into their writing lives in their respective corners of Canada, and discuss what conversations they’re looking forward to having at AWF.

With thanks to our friends at Vancouver Writers Fest.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Anna Gibbons, Patricia & Gary Holden and Julian & Lizanne Knights.

name: DISCOVERING MEDIEVAL MANUSCRIPTS IN AOTEAROA NEW ZEALAND
description:
Event 21
Discovering Medieval Manuscripts in Aotearoa New Zealand

Christopher de Hamel is probably the best-known historian of medieval manuscripts in the world. He has worked for Sotheby’s, Cambridge University, and written numerous award-winning books. However, it all began here in New Zealand, where de Hamel first encountered medieval manuscripts as a 1960s Dunedin schoolboy, followed by the libraries of Auckland and Wellington. What he found there changed his life.

The Migrants: A Memoir with Manuscripts recaptures that first thrill of childhood discovery, and traces his family’s migration to New Zealand, and the journeys of illuminated manuscripts to libraries as far from their birthplaces in Europe as it is possible to travel.

Sixty years later, he returns to where he first discovered medieval Europe in New Zealand and gives a richly illustrated lecture on both his, and the manuscripts’, remarkable journeys.

name: THE MOTHER OF ALL JOURNEYS
description:
Event 22
The Mother of all Journeys

How far would you go to create a life?

In Emma Neale’s Maybe Baby, a grieving widower takes part in an experimental medical trial to honour his late wife and raise the child they longed for.

In Elisabeth Easther’s Seed, four interlinked women navigate the complexities of fertility, pregnancy and choice as they experience how to get pregnant, stay pregnant, or avoid it altogether.

They join Anna Rawhiti-Connell to discuss how they conceived their bold novels that tackle the emotional, ethical and rarely straightforward journey of bringing new life into the world today.

name: LILY KING: TWO TRUTHS AND A LIE
description:
Event 23
Lily King: Two Truths and a Lie

Lily King is the Kirkus Prize and Whiting Award-winning author of seven books, including Euphoria and Writers & Lovers, and most recently Heart the Lover, which the New York Times Book Review hailed “her best work yet” and Booklist called “impossible to put down”.

In this masterclass King focuses on how fact and imagination can be blended to create powerful fiction.

Drawing on her many years of teaching experience in schools and American universities, she’ll guide you on the many ways lived moments (from our life or the life of another) can be blended with our imagination to tell the truest story.

A session not to be missed.

name: BARBARA DEMICK
description:
Event 24
Barbara Demick

From Nothing to Envy, her seminal book on North Korea, Eat the Buddha, which chronicled life in Tibet, to Besieged, her account of wartime Bosnia, Barbara Demick’s rigorous reporting and empathetic portraits of life in some of the world’s most closed or politically febrile places have earned her the Samuel Johnson Prize and been shortlisted for Orwell and Pulitzer prizes.

A former foreign correspondent who covered Eastern Europe, the Middle East and Asia, The Economist wrote that “few writers have done more to illuminate isolated parts of the world than Barbara Demick”.

In her first Aotearoa New Zealand appearance, she discusses her lauded career and new book Daughters of the Bamboo Grove, about twin girls separated by China’s one-child policy, with Anna Fifield.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Joséphine and Ross Green.

name: VINCENT O’MALLEY: FROM AOTEAROA NEW ZEALAND TO THE EMERALD ISLE
description:
Event 25
Vincent O’Malley: From Aotearoa New Zealand to the Emerald Isle

Vincent O’Malley is one of our leading historians, with a number of major works exploring Māori–Pākehā relations (most recently: The Meeting Place: Māori and Pākehā Encounters, 1642-1840), the New Zealand Wars and the Waikato War.

For his next project, however, he’s turning his expert eye slightly further afield – some 18,000+ km afield, in fact – to Ireland, to explore Irish immigration to Aotearoa, and the historical interactions between the Irish and Māori.

It’s a project for which O’Malley has been awarded a prestigious Judith Binney Trust Fellowship and will see him draw on archival research in New Zealand, Britain, the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland.

He meets Eugene Bingham (Pākehā, Ngāpuhi) to discuss his body of work, and give fascinating insights into his work so far on Māori and the Irish: A Unique Affinity?

Supported by the Judith Binney Trust.

name: HOW WILL I EVER GET THROUGH THIS?
description:
Event 26
How Will I Ever Get Through This?

Most people think grief only follows a death. But what about the heartbreak of a relationship ending? The loneliness of estrangement? Or the grief that accompanies illness, infertility or redundancy?

Grief expert Dr Lucy Hone (whose TED Talk about resilience and needing to draw on her own advice after the death of her 12-year-old daughter has been watched by millions) regards these as ‘living losses’.

Yet these ‘living losses’ are rarely treated with the same reverence in society, leaving those experiencing them feeling untethered, isolated and unseen.

In this interactive lecture Hone explores this silent epidemic and offers her practical tips for how we can move through life’s toughest times, and better help those around us.

name: TUSITALA TALES: ON SERVICE & SURVIVAL
description:
Event 27
Tusitala Tales: On Service & Survival

Writers from the Moana often balance professional work, communal responsibility and familial obligation alongside the demands of their craft.

Join Festival Guest Curator and poet Zech Soakai in conversation with Tangata Moana writers Leilani Tamu, Talia Stanley and To’asavili Tuputala as they reflect on what it takes to sustain a writing life within these layered responsibilities.

Through talanoa, this session explores survival, care and discipline, and reflects on how writers serve both themselves and their communities through their work.

Questions of collective identity and individual calling come to the fore, alongside the skills required to remain devoted to craft while navigating expectation, labour and love.

Part of Zech Soakai’s Guest Curatorship.

name: HE PUĀWAI: A NATURAL HISTORY OF NEW ZEALAND FLOWERS
description:
Event 28
He Puāwai: A Natural History of New Zealand Flowers

Aotearoa New Zealand has at least 2200 native species of flowering plants that have evolved in our unique conditions, and the vast majority of them grow nowhere else on earth.

In his Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted He Puāwai, internationally renowned plant diversity expert and emeritus professor of botany Philip Garnock-Jones has selected 100 native species that represent the full range of the country’s flowers: from familiar iconic flowers of kōwhai, mānuka and pōhutukawa to oddities such as the water-pollinated flowers of eelgrass, bat-pollinated blossoms of kiekie, and the world’s smallest flowers, Wolffia.

Whether you’re an avid gardener or forager, or merely curious about the nature around you, don’t miss this beautifully illustrated guide through some of our country’s smallest taonga.

Chaired by Sonya Wilson.

name: 2026 HONOURED WRITER: BILL MANHIRE
description:
Event 29
2026 Honoured Writer: Bill Manhire

A short programme blurb can’t do justice to Bill Manhire’s immense contribution to the country’s literary landscape.

He was Aotearoa New Zealand’s inaugural poet laureate, has won the Ockham NZ Book Award for Poetry five times, and founded and directed the International Institute of Modern Letters at Te Herenga Waka — Victoria University of Wellington, from which countless of the nation’s finest writers have emerged.

We are thrilled to be recognising Manhire as the Festival’s 2026 Honoured Writer, and have him join a list of luminaries including Witi Ihimaera, CK Stead, Patricia Grace, Joy Cowley, Fiona Kidman, Anne Salmond and Gavin Bishop.

While Manhire is unable to attend, Fergus Barrowman will lead a wide-ranging session about Bill's extraordinary life in letters and his major new collection, Lyrical Ballads.

name: AUTHORS AND AI: A MOMENT OF RECKONING?
description:
Event 30
Authors and AI: A Moment of Reckoning?

Each week seems to bring a new twist in the story of creative expression and intellectual property in the age of AI.

From the “human written” stickers some authors are now placing on their novels, to the debated AI-generated book covers in the Ockham NZ Book Awards, to the landmark NZ$2.6 billion settlement over the illegal downloading of books by the AI company Anthropic, the speed and scale of generative AI’s impact on creators and publishers is unprecedented.

Claire Mabey meets Karen Hao, author of the New York Times bestseller Empire of AI; leading New Zealand author Catherine Chidgey, whose work was among that scraped by Anthropic; and Sam Irvine of Copyright Licensing New Zealand to take stock of where the AI-author relationship stands today, and how writers and readers might navigate the evershifting terrain ahead.

name: THE HAPPENSTANCE HISTORIANS
description:
Event 31
The Happenstance Historians

Olivia Spooner’s gripping WWII novels (most recently The American Boys) have been mainstays of NZ’s bestseller charts and were Auckland Libraries’ most-borrowed titles of 2025.

It’s a remarkable achievement. Perhaps even more remarkable is that Spooner had no interest in history until her thirties, when an encounter with a wartime story piqued her curiosity.

For Australian author Hannah Kent, a story discovered during a teenage exchange year in Iceland became the seed for her global bestseller Burial Rites and changed her life’s course.

Ockham NZ Book Awards longlisted Tracy Farr’s Wonderland – which imagines an alternative history for a real amusement park and Marie Curie on the Miramar Peninsula – began life from an overheard remark in a café.

They discuss their gripping books and the equally fascinating paths behind them with Caroline Barron (Te Uri O Hau, Pākehā).

name: FROM EAST TO WEST (AND BACK AGAIN)
description:
Event 32
From East to West (and Back Again)

Deepa Bhasthi became the first Indian translator and the first translator of colour to win the International Booker Prize for her translation of Heart Lamp by Banu Mushtaq.

Alongside her own writing, lauded Korean author Bora Chung holds a PhD in Slavic literature and translates contemporary Russian and Polish work into Korean.

Singapore-born, Bangkok-based literary translator Shanna Tan works from Korean, Chinese, Japanese and Thai, bringing titles such as the best-selling Welcome to the Hyunamdong Bookshop by Hwang Boreum to English readers.

Daniel Hahn meets them to hear about their exceptional careers enabling literature to move between east and west, and the pleasures and challenges of doing so.

name: EMMA NEALE: DRAFTING AND CRAFTING YOUR POETRY
description:
Event 33
Emma Neale: Drafting and Crafting Your Poetry

Emma Neale is one of our most celebrated poets. She was awarded the Lauris Edmond Memorial Award for a Distinguished Contribution to New Zealand Poetry in 2020 and her sixth collection Liar, Liar, Lick, Spit won the Mary and Peter Biggs Award for Poetry at the 2025 Ockham NZ Book Awards.

In this practical and engaging workshop, Neale will help you find your way into a poem – and how to stay on course once you’ve begun.

Through guided writing exercises, you’ll generate first drafts, before turning to the art of self-editing, learning about common pitfalls that Neale encounters as an editor.

You’ll gain insights into how to craft poems that truly lift – and truly land.

name: A SHAKEN WORLD ORDER?
description:
Event 34
A Shaken World Order?

The certainties that once underpinned our geopolitical world are eroding.

Democracies are under strain, authoritarianism is on the rise, and the institutions built to maintain order have been destabilised by overlapping threats and crises, be they political, technological or environmental.

How did we get here and what lies ahead?

Our sharp panel comprised of Canadian political commentator and author of Too Dumb for Democracy? and the forthcoming On Nationalism David Moscrop, leading Indian writer and major global thinker Amitav Ghosh (Ghost-Eye) and collapse risk expert and author of Goliath’s Curse: The History and Future of Societal Collapse Luke Kemp meet Susie Ferguson to take stock, offer their predictions for the future, and discuss what we can do to shape it for the better.

name: CHARLOTTE GRIMSHAW
description:
Event 35
Charlotte Grimshaw

A woman haunted by family denial, secrets and a shadowy figure ...

In her first novel since 2018, and her first book since her interrogating memoir The Mirror Book, the protagonist of Charlotte Grimshaw’s Black Monk is confronted by her brother’s spiral into addiction, and the memories their shared past evokes.

From their chaotic Auckland childhood to her present-day life, a mysterious figure she calls the Black Monk begins to appear to her in various guises: a stranger in a cemetery; a face on television; a character surfacing in her own writing.

Part psychological thriller, part family saga, Grimshaw joins Kate De Goldi to discuss her daring new novel, which explores shame, addiction, truth and the stories we tell to survive.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Antonia Fisher & Stuart Grieve.

name: ARA: A MĀORI GUIDEBOOK OF THE MIND
description:
Event 36
Ara: A Māori Guidebook of the Mind

It’s a tumultuous time in the world, and if your inner world feels equally turbulent, you’re not alone.

Celebrated Māori psychiatrist, advocate and the best-selling author of Aroha, Wawata, Waitohu and E Moko Dear Moko, Dr Hinemoa Elder (Te Aupōuri, Ngāti Kurī, Te Rarawa, Ngāi Takoto and Ngāpuhi nui tonu) returns with Ara – a unique exploration of how to think about thinking, rooted in the wisdom of Māori culture.

Suitable for anyone seeking comfort and clarity, Elder introduces you to a profound thought process guided by Hinengaro (Māori goddess of the mind) through 23 specific rua (caves) into the depths of Papatūānuku (earth mother) and back to the surface. Through it, you’ll learn to navigate the complexities of your mind and achieve calm amid life’s noise.

Chaired by Susana Lei’ataua.

name: SALTWATER, SOLITUDE AND SONGS OF THE SEA
description:
Event 37
Saltwater, Solitude and Songs of the Sea

Set on the remote, otherworldly island of Muckle Flugga in the wild Shetland Isles, the lighthouse keeper and son of Scottish author Michael Pedersen’s eponymous novel Muckle Flugga have the fragile rhythm of their lives disrupted by a stranger’s arrival.

In Song of the Saltings by Rachael King, the isolated island of Brack’s residents live by a dark bargain: every year, a sacrifice must be made to the Glimm, the creature that haunts the salt marshes.

They join Elisabeth Easther to discuss their atmospheric novels that harness the power of island settings, and deep ancient forces of myth to create immersive coming-of-age narratives in worlds poised between old and new, lore and reality.

name: GERTRUDE STEIN AND A COMPANION
description:
Event 38
Gertrude Stein and a Companion

Gertrude Stein and a Companion by Win Wells is a glimpse into the goings-on of the greats at 27 Rue de Fleurus, the Paris salon of Gertrude Stein and Alice B. Toklas.

In a rehearsed reading of Win Wells’ acclaimed play, actors Andrea Kelland and Miranda Wilson bring to vivid life the milieu in which many of modernism’s most acclaimed works of art and literature were created.

This sharp-witted two-hander traverses the love story of Gertrude Stein and Alice B. Toklas, their lives at the heart of the modernist art movement and the circumstances that produced Stein’s enduring literary triumph The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas.

A Community Theatre production of "Gertrude Stein and a Companion" by special arrangement with Art at Work and The Play Bureau.

name: TAYARI JONES: FINDING YOUR VOICE IN A CHAOTIC WORLD
description:
Event 39
Tayari Jones: Finding Your Voice in a Chaotic World

Tayari Jones is the internationally best-selling author of five novels, including the Women’s Prize for Fiction-winning An American Marriage, and most recently, Kin – one of 2026’s most highly anticipated books according to The New York Times, The Guardian, TIME, The Financial Times and countless others.

Jones is also a hugely respected writing teacher and is currently professor of Creative Writing at Emory University and an Andrew Dickson White Professor-at-Large at Cornell University.

Don’t miss this unique chance to be in the writing room with Jones as she guides you on how to cut through the noise and chaos of our current world to find your true voice on the page.

name: YANN MARTEL
description:
Event 40
Yann Martel

Esteemed Canadian novelist Yann Martel’s Booker Prize-winning Life of Pi was a global phenomenon: selling 15 million copies worldwide, publishing in over 50 territories, spending more than a year on major bestseller charts, and adapted by Ang Lee in a four Academy Award-winning adaptation.

It was just his second novel, but it set the tone of Martel’s wider oeuvre: powerful, inventive fiction with a deeply philosophical approach to life’s big questions.

His major new novel Son of Nobody bridges the 3000-year gap between a Trojan War soldier and a modern-day Oxford scholar to explore the universality of homesickness, regret, ambition, love and grief.

Paula Morris meets him to discuss the novel expected to be one of 2026’s biggest books.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Patricia & Gary Holden.

name: BECOME UNSTOPPABLE
description:
Event 41
Become Unstoppable

In a career spanning almost three decades, visionary psychological coach Gilbert Enoka changed the way the All Blacks played their game. With a 77% winning record in test match rugby – the highest of any professional sports team – they are the only international team with a winning record against every opponent.

How? Because they treat building mindset and resilience as importantly as physical training.

Join the legendary performance coach and globally sought-after motivational speaker as he shares how elite-level sport psychology can be applied to any walk of life.

Whether in your work, home or play, Enoka’s well-honed advice on leadership, cultural upgrades and mental skills will help you master a winning mindset – whatever it is you’re setting it to.

name: TONY TULATHIMUTTE: REJECTION
description:
Event 42
Tony Tulathimutte: Rejection

“A pervert, and a madman and a stone cold genius” isn’t a way we often introduce a Festival author, but Carmen Maria Machado’s quote about major new literary star Tony Tulathimutte was too good to resist.

From his Whiting and O. Henry award-winning debut novel Private Citizens to his already cult-status novel-in-stories, Rejection, the Thai-American writer has emerged as one of the sharpest, funniest and most incisive writers about modern life, internet culture, identity and relationships today.

Featuring the viral short story “The Feminist”, and following interlinked lives of characters derailed by rejection of some form, it was hailed as “startlingly good” by The Wall Street Journal and “the year’s feel-bad book” (complimentarily) by The Boston Globe.

Victor Rodger meets him.

name: SMALL TOWNS, DARK TRUTHS
description:
Event 43
Small Towns, Dark Truths

From the Far North to the wild West Coast, Fiona Sussman meets three authors of gripping crime thrillers in distinctly NZ settings: Zoë Rankin (The Vanishing Place), Chris Blake (Softly Calls the Devil) and Catherine Lea (The Deeper the Dead).

What makes these isolated towns in some of NZ’s most remote spots such fertile ground for these crime writers?

Which unique characteristics of their settings did they draw upon to create their atmospheric and menacing works?

And, when depicting real people and places (particularly in close-knit communities), how did they navigate the line between fact and fiction?

Join them to find out.

name: A BIG NIGHT OUT FOR LITTLE BOOKWORMS
description:
Event 44
A Big Night Out for Little Bookworms

The adult programme kicks off with an opening night gala, but why should the grown-ups get all the fun? Back by very popular demand, our big opening night for the smallest bookworms returns in a fast-paced night of thrills, joy and creative chaos.

Watch an epic illustration battle unfold, see a brand-new story created live from the audience’s wildest ideas, cheer on a kids-versus-adults book quiz, watch a scene from Betsy Balloon and get silly with a joyfully rowdy reading of Pūkeko Who-keko? Featuring Claire Mabey, Toby Morris, Zak Waipara (Ngāti Porou, Rongowhakaata, Ngāti Ruapani, Ngāti Kahungunu) and Daniel Hahn. Hosted by comedy queen, Johanna Cosgrove.

All done before bedtime!

Best suited for ages 5+.

name: IAN MCEWAN
description:
Event 45
Ian McEwan

Called “the supreme novelist of his generation” (The Sunday Times), Ian McEwan is the critically acclaimed author of 19 novels, including the Booker Prize-winning Amsterdam, The Child in Time, Saturday and Lessons, as well as Atonement, Enduring Love, The Children Act and On Chesil Beach, all of which have received major screen adaptations.

Speaking with Simon Wilson in this special interview live from his home in the UK, he discusses his landmark new novel What We Can Know, which envisions a 2119 submerged by rising seas, and a society living with the consequence of climate inaction.

Literary critic Johanna Thomas-Corr described it as “a postapocalyptic campus novel … AS Byatt’s Possession crossed with Cormac McCarthy’s The Road. Dark academia meets the big ideas novel” and McEwan’s “most richly layered work”.

A conversation not to miss.

Supported by Platinum Patron Kevin Ramsbottom-Isherwood.

name: FLORENCE KNAPP: THE NAMES
description:
Event 46
Florence Knapp: The Names

It is 1987, and in the aftermath of a great storm, a woman sets out to register her son’s birth. Her abusive husband expects her to follow family tradition and call the baby after him.

But is it right for her child to inherit his name from generations of domineering men?

So begins Florence Knapp’s The Names, which imagines three different life paths prompted by three different names, and reflects on the decisions – big and small – that shape a life’s course.

Full of heartbreak and hope, it was deemed “the best debut novel in years” by The Sunday Times, praised by Jojo Moyes, Chris Whitaker and Geraldine Brooks, and has barely left Auckland’s bookshop and library charts.

Michèle A’Court meets her.

name: MIEKO KAWAKAMI
description:
Event 47
Mieko Kawakami

Mieko Kawakami is one of Japan’s most distinguished and internationally acknowledged writers.

Breasts and Eggs won Japan’s leading literary prize, the Akutagawa, and was recognised as a New York Times Notable Book of the Year and one of TIME’s Best 10 Books of 2020.

Her second novel to appear in English, Heaven, was shortlisted for the International Booker Prize 2022 and her third, All the Lovers in the Night, appeared on the US’ prestigious National Book Critics Award shortlist.

She makes her much-anticipated return with Sisters in Yellow: a masterpiece of teenage dreams and adult cruelties; enduring friendship and deep betrayal.

Kiran Dass meets her to discuss her outstanding body of work in her first Aotearoa New Zealand appearance.

Interpretation provided by Melanie Taylor.

name: MICK HERRON
description:
Event 48
Mick Herron

Described as “the John le Carré of his generation” (Val McDermid) and a “megastar of the genre” (The Guardian), spy writer Mick Herron has earned millions of fans and delighted critics worldwide with the thrilling plots, rich cast of characters and dry humour of his Slough House series.

Named for the outpost where disgraced spies (the ‘slow horses’) are banished to see out the rest of their derailed careers, the success of the series has been nothing short of phenomenal.

It has sold over four million copies, been translated into more than 25 languages, and seen Herron be awarded the Theakston Old Peculier Crime Novel of the

Year and the Diamond Dagger award, which recognises outstanding lifetime achievement in crime writing.

A multi-BAFTA award-winning television adaptation, Slow Horses, starring Gary Oldman as Jackson Lamb, has brought a whole new legion of fans.

Making his Aotearoa New Zealand debut, he joins Jim Mora to discuss his unique style of espionage and political thriller, what has shaped him as a writer, and introduce his newest instalment: Clown Town.

name: DOWN THE RABBITHOLE
description:
Event 49
Down the Rabbithole

“Going down the rabbit hole” usually means you’ve reached weird corners of the internet, or lost several hours to the whims of YouTube’s algorithm.

The perk of being a writer? You get to claim this as an integral part of the creative process.

Whether we buy that or not, don’t miss Festival writers lining up to share an unexpected topic – however niche, quirky or downright odd – that led them down the proverbial rabbit hole, and ended up being significant to their work.

Hosted by Jo Randerson and featuring SA Cosby, Tracy Farr, Daniel Hahn and Josie Shapiro.

name: SĀMOAN MADE SIMPLE – GREETINGS AND COMMON PHRASES
description:
Event 50
Sāmoan Made Simple – Greetings and Common Phrases

Start your day with language teacher Jason Tiatia as he guides you through some of the basics of the Sāmoan language with fun, buildable exercises for all levels.

name: INDUSTRY INSIGHTS WITH THE BIG IDEA: PUBLISHER EDITION
description:
Event 51
Industry Insights with The Big Idea: Publisher Edition

Bite-sized interviews between The Big Idea Editor Gabi Lardies and practitioners and professionals in the NZ literary sector. Today she sits down with publishers to demystify the publishing process.

name: WORLDWIDE BOOK CLUB: POLITICS OF TRANSLATION
description:
Event 52
Worldwide Book Club: Politics of Translation

Literary translator Daniel Hahn and Sumaia Daoud and Betty Davis from the Pōneke-based collective

Al-Rifaq Translations discuss their recent projects through the lens of translation’s political value.

name: DEBUT OF THE DAY: JOSEPH TRINIDAD
description:
Event 53
Debut of the Day: Joseph Trinidad

Not many debut authors can boast a blurb from Alexander Chee. Discover why he calls Joseph Trinidad “an entirely original voice” as Joseph reads from and discusses his remarkable essay collection Lucky Creatures.

name: NO STUPID QUESTIONS: DAVID MOSCROP
description:
Event 54
No Stupid Questions: David Moscrop

Back by popular demand, a chance to put your current affairs quandaries to a world expert. Canadian political commentator David Moscrop steps into the hot seat.

name: MARIA REVA: CREATING CHARACTER NETWORKS
description:
Event 55
Maria Reva: Creating Character Networks

Imagine creating characters who share among them powerful magnetic-like forces fuelled by needs and desires, knowledge and power. With a character network in a narrative ecosystem, the closer they are drawn together, the greater the dramatic tension.

Join 2025 Booker Prize-longlisted author of Endling Maria Reva in this unique masterclass as she guides you through this technique and how to apply it to your creative project.

In preparation, attendees are asked to read two provided stories from Reva’s lauded story collection, Good Citizens Need Not Fear (“Letter of Apology” and "Bone Music") and in the session she will share the original ambitions for the stories, why they didn’t work on the page, and how she evolved them in the drafting process.

name: WITI IHIMAERA: SWIMMING HOME
description:
Event 56
Witi Ihimaera: Swimming Home

“I have reset my compass. I have started at the beginning again,” writes Witi Ihimaera (Te Whānau a Kai, Te Aitanga a Māhaki, Rongowhakaata, Ngāti Ira, Ngāti Porou) in his memoir Te Kaikaukau | The Swimmer: I te Ao o te Reo, which traces his unique journey of learning his own language at the age of 80.

His year in the full-immersion course became an odyssey of profound self-discovery – of loss and shame, of reconnection and wairua, of fathers and teachers, kuia and friends.

He joins Stacey Morrison (Te Arawa, Ngāi Tahu) in conversation and with musical components provided by the talented Kingsley Spargo.

Don’t miss this moving session with our most acclaimed Māori writer reflecting on identity, belonging and finding himself anew in his ninth decade.

name: GOLIATH’S CURSE: THE HISTORY AND FUTURE OF SOCIETAL COLLAPSE
description:
Event 57
Goliath’s Curse: The History and Future of Societal Collapse

For academic Luke Kemp, our modern civilisation is exceptionally fragile. Collapse isn’t inevitable, but we need radical change to prevent it – and history shows a cautionary pattern.

Surveying 5000 years of human history, and over 400 civilisation collapses – from the Bronze Age to modern times – Kemp highlights a recurring trend: elites unsustainably seize and concentrate resources; inequality grows to destabilising levels and destroys said society.

Essentially, a “Goliath’s Curse” – the very features that make a society powerful become its undoing.

Today? The fossil fuel industry, big tech and military industrial complexes rule our world, and pose new threats of annihilating us – from climate change to nuclear war.

With Julia Wheeler he discusses what lessons from history might save our future.

name: IF THIS BE MAGIC: SHAKESPEARE IN TRANSLATION
description:
Event 58
If This Be Magic: Shakespeare in Translation

How do you rebuild a 400-year-old topical joke in a new culture and with none of the same words? How does Romeo and Juliet’s first meeting unfold if they can’t mention pilgrims? What does Taming of the Shrew sound like in a language where every noun has a gender? Why are Lady Macbeth’s pronouns such a problem?

Seasoned translator and Shakespeare fanatic Daniel Hahn has travelled the globe to uncover how Shakespeare has moved between languages, and the pleasures, challenges and oddities each new translation brings.

From Thai to te reo, Swedish to Swahili, Hahn joins Kate De Goldi to discuss his epic project and resulting book, which Katherine Rundell describes as “like attending the most fascinating lecture of the term”.

name: OCEANS BETWEEN US: PACIFIC PEOPLES & RACISM IN AOTEAROA
description:
Event 59
Oceans Between Us: Pacific Peoples & Racism in Aotearoa

Toni Morrison observed that racism distracts people from their work by forcing them into a constant state of self-explanation.

Join Guest Curator Zech Soakai in conversation with Dr Sereana Naepi, editor of the landmark collection Oceans Between Us: Pacific Peoples and Racism in Aotearoa, for an urgent and incisive discussion about how racism continues to shape Pacific experiences in our country today, and what it means to speak back from a Moana-centred perspective.

Grounded in Dr Naepi’s scholarship and encompassing perspectives from 13 expert contributors across education, health, climate and justice, together they reflect on structural racism, lived experience, and the possibilities for clarity, resistance and collective imagining among Tangata Moana in the Aotearoa diaspora.

Part of Zech Soakai’s Guest Curatorship.

name: BETSY BALLOON
description:
Event 60
Betsy Balloon

Welcome to the island of Helium where Betsy lives with her balloon factory-owning family.

They’re cheerful, a bit money-mad and life is on the up. But when the world turns against balloons, their world pops and Betsy must start a new life in a faraway city with her eccentric uncle, Hercules Snark, an inventor of mostly half-finished things.

Unfortunately, Snark has problems of his own. In his city, it never stops raining. The streets are flooding. And things – quite literally – are starting to sink.

Betsy Balloon is a rambunctious modern fable presented as a staged reading with original music and illustration based on a story by this year’s Honoured Writer, Bill Manhire.

Best suited for ages 5-10 years.

name: BARBARA DEMICK: COVERING REPRESSIVE REGIMES
description:
Event 61
Barbara Demick: Covering Repressive Regimes

How do journalists do their work while covering countries such as China, North Korea, Iran, Russia – and increasingly the United States under the Trump administration?

As authoritarian regimes hone their means of control, journalists have to up their game.

In this unique masterclass, Samuel Johnson Prize winner and Orwell and Pulitzer Prize shortlisted journalist Barbara Demick (Daughters of the Bamboo Grove, Eat the Buddha, Nothing to Envy) reveals some of the techniques modern journalists employ: from defector interviews, to open source intelligence, to social media.

Don’t miss one of the world’s leading journalists sharing tricks of the trade used to bring stories from some of the globe’s most restricted areas to light.

name: EMPIRE OF AI: THE RECKLESS RACE FOR DOMINATION
description:
Event 62
Empire of AI: The Reckless Race for Domination

When leading AI journalist Karen Hao began covering OpenAI in 2019, she thought they were the good guys.

Founded as a non-profit devoted to ensuring that artificial general intelligence (AGI) would benefit all of humanity, the mission rapidly gave way to commercial pressures, peer competition and fierce hoarding of resources.

In Empire of AI – which became an instant New York Times bestseller and saw her recognised on the TIME100 AI List – Hao explores OpenAI’s meteoric rise and its astonishing human, material and environmental impact.

And, with its seemingly relentless extraction of resources, labour and land largely from the Global South, Hao likens the industry to colonial empires of old.

She joins Dr Kerry McInerney (Ngāti Ruanui) to discuss her ground-breaking book.

Supported by Platinum Patron Theresa Gattung.

name: FAIL AGAIN. FAIL BETTER?
description:
Event 63
Fail Again. Fail Better?

“Failure” isn’t the word you’d naturally attribute to multi-million copy best-selling espionage writer Mick Herron or double Jann Medlicott Acorn Fiction Prize-winning novelist Catherine Chidgey.

But success – much like life – is not always linear.

Herron’s journey to the top of the charts was a circuitous one, and his love of writing about life’s rejects and underdogs fuelled his Slough House series, now in a BAFTA-winning TV adaptation, Slow Horses.

Chidgey had a 13-year gap between her first three published novels, and her extraordinary return with The Wish Child, Remote Sympathy, The Axeman’s Carnival, Pet and The Book of Guilt.

They join Petra Bagust to discuss navigating life’s harder parts, and how the twists and turns have shaped them into the writers they are today.

name: RETELLING FEMALE HISTORIES
description:
Event 64
Retelling Female Histories

Across centuries and continents, women’s stories have been suppressed, distorted, or omitted altogether from the history books.

Carole Beu meets three authors writing them back in.

Whether in her fiction or biographies, acclaimed French writer Alexandra Lapierre has brought great women neglected by history to life – most recently Stella Miles Franklin.

Irish-Indian poet and playwright Nikita Gill’s work recentres women in folklore and Greek and Hindu myth. Her latest work is an electrifying retelling of the Greek goddess Hekate.

In Fulvia, NZ talent Kaarina Parker (Ngāpuhi) resurrects an exceptional Roman noblewoman from history’s margins.

Together they discuss the drive, joys and challenges – as well as the years of hard work and research – behind bringing these women into the light.

name: BETWEEN DREAMS: RESISTANCE AND REPRESENTATION IN ASIAN AOTEAROA
description:
Event 65
Between Dreams: Resistance and Representation in Asian Aotearoa

“Dreaming is an active state,” writes Grace Gassin in Between Dreams. “It is a time when we engage with our past and play with the ingredients of our potential futures.”

It is also an act of bravery – and generative. When protestors took to the streets in Hong Kong, they referred to their demonstrations as “dreaming”, while the Cantonese verb for dreaming is “發夢 (faat3mung6)”, implying that a dream spreads, radiates and proliferates.

Spanning diverse heritages and professions, the contributors to this landmark anthology represent the dreams of many people between Asia and Aotearoa.

Contributors Rebekah Jaung, Tze Ming Mok and Balamohan Shingade join Nathan Joe to examine how Asian–New Zealanders are continually remaking what is means to be Asian in Aotearoa today.

name: NOELLE MCCARTHY: SHAPING YOUR STORY, ADDING VOICES TO YOUR OWN
description:
Event 66
Noelle McCarthy: Shaping Your Story, Adding Voices to Your Own

One of the hardest things about memoir writing is deciding what to put in and what to leave out.

In this masterclass, award-winning memoirist and long-form podcast maker Noelle McCarthy considers the crucial judgment at the heart of first-person creative writing: how much of your life – and the people in it – can and should you include? And how best to incorporate these perspectives and navigate all the contradictions, gaps and questions that subjective rememberings (including the writer’s own!) bring?

Together you’ll cover key elements of crafting long-form works of personal history including: identifying subjects, ethical considerations, effective interviewing and crafting dialogue.

Suitable for anyone considering memoir writing, or writers already underway and interested in adding other perspectives into their work.

name: WRITING IN TODAY’S AMERICA
description:
Event 67
Writing in Today’s America

Barely an hour, nay minute, goes by without the US being the subject of a major news story.

The early weeks of 2026 alone have seen a military invasion of Venezuela; a threat to seize Greenland; fatal ICE shootings in Minneapolis; severe winter storms and a fractured Davos where President Trump was accused of rupturing the world order.

A country’s writers are often looked towards to make sense of things. What is the daily reality of being a writer in contemporary America right now? And whether writing fiction or non-fiction, how are writers keeping pace and making sense of this unprecedented chapter in their nation’s story?

Toby Manhire meets major American writer and Minneapolis bookshop owner Louise Erdrich, crime author SA Cosby and non-fiction author Deborah Baker to discuss.

name: CHARLOTTE MCCONAGHY
description:
Event 68
Charlotte McConaghy

Charlotte McConaghy is the New York Times best-selling author of Once There Were Wolves, Migrations and most recently, Wild Dark Shore, which begins with a woman washing up on a research station on a remote island halfway between New Zealand and Antarctica.

It was an instant New York Times bestseller, a Time Must-Read for 2025 and selected for Reese Witherspoon’s Book Club – and the queue for it in Auckland’s library network has been 500+ strong for months.

In her first visit to the Festival she sits down with Sonya Wilson to reflect on her lauded writing career to date, and the gripping, propulsive literary thriller that has brought her to the attention of a host of new readers.

name: MARIA REVA
description:
Event 69
Maria Reva

An ‘endling’ is the last living individual of a species. Once that individual dies, it’s the end of the line.

Ukrainian-Canadian author Maria Reva’s 2025 Booker Prize-longlisted Endling blends the plight of an endangered snail, women in the Ukrainian romance tourism industry, and civilian lives in Ukraine and beyond during war – all told with urgency and humour.

Intrigued? You should be.

Ann Patchett called it “essential reading”; the Booker Prize judges deemed it “unforgettable” and Percival Everett said “[i]t’s about so much and yet is laser focused … I love works that are smarter than I am and this is one”.

With Jenna Todd (Kāi Tahu) she discusses her novel that has received international praise, and been championed enthusiastically by many Auckland booksellers.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Julian & Lizanne Knights.

name: THE CHIEF AND THE EMPIRE
description:
Event 70
The Chief and the Empire

The Chief and the Empire by Eugene Bingham (Pākehā, Ngāpuhi) uncovers the extraordinary true tale of Te Tai Tokerau rangatira Te Pahi – the first influential Māori leader to cross the Tasman – whose curiosity about the Pākehā world forged alliances, saved lives, and ultimately cost him his own.

Part history, part true-crime investigation, it is a riveting account of Te Pahi’s journey and early Māori–Pākehā encounters, the injustice that destroyed a leader, and the unexpected legacy carried by the descendants of the men he fought to protect.

The lauded journalist, writer and producer known for bringing remarkable stories to light meets Annabelle Lee-Mather (Ngāi Tahu, Ngāti Kahungunu, Ngāti Māmoe) to discuss this incredible tale that shaped Aotearoa and Australia, and what went into its telling.

name: WAIATA IN AN HOUR
description:
Event 71
Waiata in an Hour

Kia hono mai ki tētahi hanga mīharo i te kaipuoro, i a Laughton Kora (Ngāi Tūhoe, Ngāti Pūkeko) e whakatuwhera nei i tana tukanga auaha me te tono i te minenga kia tū hei wāhanga mō te waihangatanga, mō te tuhinga, me te whakaaturanga o tētahi waiata e hou katoa ana i roto i te 60 meneti.

Mā te minenga te momo puoro me ngā kupu matua e whakatakoto ki mua i a Laughton. Ka rere ana te ine-wā, ka rere ngā mahi a Laughton.

Haria mai tō taonga puoro, āpitihia tō reo kia whai wāhi ki te korihi e kīia nei ko te ‘doo-wop’,āpitihia tō ringa kia whai manawataki ai a Laughton, me noho noa rānei kia rangona ai te ngahau o te puoro e hua mai ana.

Kei mahue tēnei ara hou e kitea ai tā te taonga puoro para i te ara o te tukanga tuhituhi nā runga i tētahi ara me uaua ka kitea e te tangata.

Ka rauhītia e Michael rāua ko Matariki Bennett (nō Ngāti Pikiao, nō Ngāti Whakaue hoki).

Be part of something special as musician Laughton Kora (Ngāi Tūhoe, Ngāti Pūkeko) opens his creative process and invites the audience to

be part of conceiving, writing and performing an entirely original waiata in just 60 minutes.

The audience gives Laughton the genre and a key lyric. The timer starts and Laughton goes to work.

Bring your taonga puoro, bring your voices to be part of a doo-wop chorus, bring your hand to give Laughton a backing beat or simply sit back and enjoy the magic unfolding.

Don’t miss this unique opportunity for a performing taonga to lift the curtain on a writing process in a way that few people will ever get a chance to see.

Curated by Michael and Matariki Bennett (Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Whakaue).

name: RF KUANG
description:
Event 72
RF Kuang

It’s hard to think of a young writer as accomplished as RF Kuang (the Poppy War trilogy, Babel, Yellowface and Katabasis).

She’s a #1 New York Times and #1 Sunday Times bestseller; has won the Nebula, Locus, Crawford and British Book Awards; been recognised on the Time ‘100 Next’ and Forbes ‘30 Under 30’ lists, and holds degrees from Cambridge and Oxford, with a Yale PhD in progress – all by the tender age of 29.

Don’t miss Rosabel Tan meeting this exceptionally talented writer whose most recent novel, Katabasis (Ancient Greek for a hero’s descent to the underworld), is a dark academia blockbuster of two Cambridge rivals who make the ultimate journey to protect their careers: to hell.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Lizzie Philip & Andrew Faulkner

name: HOW TO BE A GOOD ANCESTOR
description:
Event 73
How to be a Good Ancestor

Ko te tūāpapa o tēnei pae, ko mātāpono taketake o te whai whakaaro ki ngā reanga e whitu kei mua i te aroaro, ā, ka kotahi mai ki tēnei pae ngā mātānga ka hōpara i He Whakaputanga me TeTiriti i te pōtitanga e kainamu mai ana i tēnei whenua.

Ka tūtaki a Gabriella Brayne (nō Ngāti Maniapoto, he Falefa, he Pākehā) ki te kiritohe, ki te kaituhi o Mana, ki a Tāme Iti (nō Ngāi Tūhoe), ki te mātanga pūmātauranga, ki te kaituhi o The State of Māori Rights, ki a Professor Margaret Mutu (nō Ngāti Kahu, nō Te Rarawa, nō Ngāti Whātua), me te Ahorangi o ngā Tōrangapū, me te kaituhi o The Good Settler, me Richard Shaw (he Pākehā) i a rātou e huritao ana ki tō tātou ahunga, ki te tāraitanga me te werohanga o ngā mana whakahaere, me ngā ara e tūmanako tonu ai te tangata i ēnei wā e nui nei ngā wero o te wā.

He aha ngā kōwhiringa o ēnei rā ka tārai i te ao o ngā uri whakaheke?

He Wāhanga nō tā Shilo Kino Noho hei Kairauhī Manuhiri.

Grounded in the Indigenous principle of thinking seven generations ahead, this panel brings together expert voices to explore He Whakaputanga and Te Tiriti as the country approaches an election.

Gabriella Brayne (Ngāti Maniapoto, Falefa, Pākehā) meets renowned activist and author of Mana Tāme Iti (Ngāi Tūhoe), scholar and author of The State of Māori Rights Professor Margaret Mutu (Ngāti Kahu, Te Rarawa, Ngāti Whātua) and Professor of politics and author of The Good Settler Richard Shaw (Pākehā) as they reflect on where we are heading, how power is shaped and contested, and what it takes to hold onto hope in challenging times.

What choices are we making today that will shape the lives of those who will come after us?

Part of Shilo Kino’s Guest Curatorship.

This session will be held in English.

name: NZ'S ECONOMY: WHERE NEXT?
description:
Event 74
NZ's Economy: Where Next?

New Zealanders are leaving in their droves (2025 was the lowest net migration gain for the country since 2013) and for many of those left, life is feeling tougher than ever.

There are green shoots showing, but it feels fragile. Is the current economic recovery recipe the right one?

Anna Rawhiti-Connell meets a crack team of deep thinkers about the economy to hear their thoughts, and put your questions to them.

Joining her are economist, Director of Policy at the New Zealand Council of Trade Unions and former government Senior Advisor Craig Renney (The Good Economy), award-winning journalist Rebecca Macfie (Hardship and Hope, Pakukore) and leading personal finance expert Mary Holm (Rich Enough?).

Together they take stock of the country’s position and answer your pressing questions.

name: DEEPA BHASTHI: TRANSLATING WITH AN ACCENT
description:
Event 75
Deepa Bhasthi: Translating with an Accent

Winner of the 2025 International Booker Prize, Heart Lamp by Banu Mushtaq compiles 12 stories – largely of women confronting patriarchy, class, caste and religion in South India – and was originally written and published in the Kannada language.

Carefully translated into English by accomplished writer Deepa Bhasthi, its win marked the first time an Indian translator has won the prestigious prize and brought the collection to worldwide attention.

Chair of the judges Max Porter described Bhasthi’s translation as “ruffl[ing] language to create new textures in a plurality of Englishes” and that “it challenges and expands our understanding of translation”.

Spoor Books co-founder Balamohan Shingade meets her for a powerful discussion of the politics of language, and her unique approach to translation.

name: CLEAN WRITING WITH TONY TULATHIMUTTE
description:
Event 76
Clean Writing with Tony Tulathimutte

When we talk about a writer’s style, we usually talk about the personal flourishes that make them recognisable and distinct from other writers.

But some style considerations run deeper than that, and are useful no matter what kind of writer you are.

This masterclass led by the Whiting and O. Henry-winning author Tony Tulathimutte (Private Citizens, Rejection) will explore three of them: clichés, word choice and concision.

Mastering these concepts will rid you of unnecessary and derivative language, leaving just your own.

name: AMITAV GHOSH
description:
Event 77
Amitav Ghosh

Amitav Ghosh is one of India’s most decorated and internationally recognised writers.

At once a genre-defying novelist, a major postcolonial thinker and one of literature’s most influential voices on climate change, his diverse body of work has earned him India’s highest literary honour, two lifetime achievement awards and seen him be named by Foreign Policy as one of the decade’s most important global thinkers.

In 2025 he also became the 12th writer – joining the likes of Margaret Atwood, David Mitchell and Ocean Vuong – to submit a manuscript to Norway’s Future Library project, to be locked away until 2114.

Simon Wilson meets him to reflect on an impressive oeuvre, and introduce Ghost-Eye, his new novel which explores memory, reincarnation and our planet’s fragility.

name: SA COSBY
description:
Event 78
SA Cosby

Described by Michael Connelly as “always hit[ting] the grand slam of crime fiction”, “sensationally good” by Lee Child, and “exhilarating” by Stephen King, SA Cosby has quickly become one of crime fiction’s hottest talents.

Combining action-fuelled plots and literary depth to explore race, class, family, justice and redemption in the American South, his unique blend of Southern Noir has made him a mainstay on “Books of the Year” round-ups, Barack Obama’s reading list and The New York Times bestsellers.

Whether you’re already a Cosby convert, or just discovering him, don’t miss him as he joins leading NZ crime writer Michael Bennett (Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Whakaue) to discuss his life, writing and newest book King of Ashes – a blistering, Godfather-inspired family saga.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Susan & Gavin Walker.

name: HOPE IN THE MACHINE
description:
Event 79
Hope in the Machine

From human data cleaning centres in the Global South to AI companies’ ruthless race for resources, Karen Hao’s New York Times best-selling Empire of AI is a sobering read.

However, it ends on a note of hope. Right here in Aotearoa.

Hao introduces us to Te Hiku Media’s Peter-Lucas Jones (Te Aupōuri, Ngāi Takoto, Ngāti Kahu) and Keoni Mahelona (Kanaka Māoli) and their groundbreaking use of AI technology to protect and revitalise tikanga and te reo Māori.

In stark contrast to Silicon Valley’s scale-at-all costs ethos, Te Hiku builds AI grounded in Māori data sovereignty – with consent, reciprocity and kaitiakitanga guiding every development stage.

They meet in person for the first time to discuss this ethical, community-led use of AI, and whether it holds the blueprint for a different kind of AI world.

name: NEW ZEALAND GOTHIC
description:
Event 80
New Zealand Gothic

While we may be lacking the castles and abbeys of the gothic tradition, two exciting NZ authors, Laura Borrowdale and Laura Vincent (Ngāti Māhanga, Ngāpuhi), make a strong case for a New Zealand version of the genre with their latest books.

Vincent describes her Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted debut novel Hoods Landing as a “Southern Gothic–via–rural South Auckland anti-tragedy” and the horrors of domestic banality in Ōtautahi Christchurch and wider NZ are the foundation for Borrowdale’s unsettling sophomore short story collection Dead Ends.

They meet Emma Gleason to discuss the quiet unease in their books, and their thoughts on what is distinctly “New Zealand” about their brand of gothic.

name: THAMMIKA SONGKAEO: MIGRATION AND MOTHERHOOD
description:
Event 81
Thammika Songkaeo: Migration and Motherhood

Join Leilani Tamu in conversation with transnational Thai writer Thammika Songkaeo for an exploration of her compelling debut novel Stamford Hospital, which dissects motherhood, marriage and the cost of selfhood.

Set in Singapore, the novel moves through spaces of illness, labour, desire and migration, tracing its narrator’s profound sense of isolation within the very institutions– marriage, motherhood, home – that are meant to sustain her.

This session explores the novel’s key themes of care, power and intimacy and considers Songkaeo’s approach to writing across borders and languages.

Expect a thoughtful exchange that invites readers into the novel’s layered world and its searching questions about home, vulnerability, and how local stories connect via universal experiences.

Part of Zech Soakai’s Guest Curatorship.

name: ELIZABETH KNOX: PLAUSIBILITY, TONE AND TRUTH
description:
Event 82
Elizabeth Knox: Plausibility, Tone and Truth

How to tell a story that will be believed?

Join internationally acclaimed author Elizabeth Knox (The Vintner’s Luck, The Absolute Book and most recently, Night, Ma) to learn how to build a world so that its parts fit and move together.

Elizabeth will offer strategies to help writers discover what they need, and need to know, to make a believable fictional setting and to start the engine of their plot, without cheats or conveniences, and with room for happy accidents.

Come prepared to share what is tripping you up in your work. Exercises will be based on getting your character into trouble, and how to worry your reader (this last one very much applies to writing non-fiction as well!).

name: TAYARI JONES
description:
Event 83
Tayari Jones

Vernice and Annie are ‘cradle friends’, both born in Louisiana, both destined never to know their mothers. The girls are inseparable, but as they grow up, their lives start to look very different in the segregated America of the 1950s and 60s.

Such is the premise of Tayari Jones’ highly anticipated new novel Kin: her first since her Women’s Prize for Fiction-winning An American Marriage, which became an instant New York Times bestseller and was championed by Oprah and Barack Obama.

In her first Aotearoa New Zealand appearance, she joins Dione Joseph to discuss her remarkable writing journey and Kin, which Ann Patchett described as “the kind of all-encompassing reading experience I’m always hoping to find: smart and funny and deftly profound”.

name: WHO GETS TO TELL THE STORY?
description:
Event 84
Who Gets to Tell the Story?

Yellowface by RF Kuang and Fruit Fly by Josh Silver both pose powerful questions about who gets to tell the story, who profits from it, and the dark consequences of ambition and greed in the world of publishing.

In Yellowface, Kuang’s white protagonist hits literary stardom after stealing her Asian American friend’s manuscript, crafting a false life that rapidly spirals out of control.

In Silver’s Fruit Fly, a privileged writer looking for a zeitgeisty subject to cure her writer’s block collides with a young, queer struggling addict with dramatic consequences.

They join Victor Rodger to discuss their sharp, propulsive novels that expose tokenism, exploitation and the fraught ethics of telling another’s story.

name: ART IN THE TIME OF WAR
description:
Event 85
Art in the Time of War

What does it mean to be a writer whose homeland – or generational homeland – is at the heart of world headlines?

The Booker Prize-longlisted Endling by Ukrainian-Canadian author Maria Reva began as a novel about the Ukrainian marriage industry until Russia’s invasion of Ukraine disrupted Reva’s writing process and, indeed, the novel itself.

Palestinian scholar Tareq Baconi’s memoir Fire in Every Direction traces both his queer awakening and generations of his family’s displacement through war, reaching readers at a crucial moment in Palestine’s history.

What is the writer’s responsibility to document these moments in their country’s histories? And with their homes now spanning Vancouver and London, how do they navigate living here, writing there?

Chaired by Julia Wheeler.

name: BORA CHUNG
description:
Event 86
Bora Chung

Ask critics or readers to name top Korean contemporary writers, and Bora Chung’s name would come up within seconds.

Her unique, unsettling blend of speculative fiction, horror, folktales and societal critique has seen her shortlisted for the International Booker Prize and National Book Award with Cursed Bunny, and Your Utopia was nominated for the Philip K. Dick Award.

She returns with The Midnight Timetable, a novel-in-ghost-stories that is equal parts bone-chilling, wryly funny and deeply political.

And in a fascinating other thread to Chung’s work, she holds a PhD in Slavic literature and translates contemporary Russian and Polish literature into Korean.

Paula Morris meets Chung in her Aotearoa New Zealand debut appearance to hear about her diverse work across genres and languages.

name: PAX ASSADI: MORTIFIED
description:
Event 87
Pax Assadi: Mortified

Pax Assadi’s infectious stand-up comedy has seen him nominated for some of the industry’s biggest awards and appear on major shows such as 7 Days and Taskmaster NZ.

And he can now add author to his already impressive resumé.

His debut memoir Mortified is hilarious (unsurprisingly), and painfully honest as he reflects on his childhood and teenage years growing up as the son of refugees in Auckland.

Interrogating the memories he’s tried to keep in the dark, he examines when does – and should – a painful experience become “good material”?

He retraces his story with the same vulnerability and warmth that infuses his comedy in conversation with Tim Provise (Te Āti Awa o Taranaki).

name: HELEN GARNER: HOW TO END A STORY
description:
Event 88
Helen Garner: How to End a Story

From her bold fiction Monkey Grip, The Children’s Bach and The Spare Room, to her debate-starting non-fiction, The First Stone, This House of Grief and The Mushroom Tapes, Helen Garner has indelibly shaped Australian Literature for 50 years and emerged as one of our age’s greatest writers.

But it is her diaries Garner regards as her favourite – and she’s not alone. David Nicholls, Nigella Lawson, The Times Literary Editor and Dua Lipa all assembled to praise her era-spanning collected diaries, How to End a Story.

In late 2025, they became the first diaries ever to win the Baillie Gifford Prize, with judges comparing her to Virginia Woolf.

Emily Perkins joins Garner to discuss a stellar writing career, and how to capture that most quietly remarkable thing: a life.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Pip Greenwood and David Gibson.

name: TĀME ITI: MANA
description:
Event 89
Tāme Iti: Mana

For more than five decades, Tāme Iti (Ngāi Tūhoe) has stood at the heart of Aotearoa New Zealand’s struggle for Indigenous rights.

From land marches to performance art, police raids to prison cells, his voice has challenged the country to reckon with its colonial legacy.

Once branded as a dangerous and extreme activist, now hailed as a national treasure, Tāme has lived the contradictions and realities of standing with mana motuhake in a modern world.

After being silenced from speaking te reo Māori as a child, Tāme championed its revitalisation and discovering the power of protest set him on a remarkable path.

With Eugene Bingham (Pākehā, Ngāpuhi), he discusses capturing an extraordinary life in his memoir Mana, and reflects on what it means to live with mana in a world that often tries to strip it away.

name: GRIEVING TOGETHER: TUSIATA AVIA AND NAFANUA PURCELL KERSEL
description:
Event 90
Grieving Together: Tusiata Avia and Nafanua Purcell Kersel

My father has been chipping himself down since he arrived here, he is half man and half vessel, readying for the journey to Hawaiki.

Prime Minister’s Award for Literary Achievement and Ockham NZ Book Award-winner Tusiata Avia’s new collection Giving Birth to My Father follows her father as he travels through his last days and into the arms of his tupu’aga.

The poems of Nafanua Purcell Kersel’s Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted debut collection Black Sugarcane span light and dark, quiet and loud, calm and violence, and include a moving sequence on the devastating 2009 tsunami that struck between the Samoan islands of Upolu and Tutuila.

These phenomenal poets join Susana Lei’ataua to discuss capturing unimaginable grief – whether individual or collective – and the immense identity shifts grief brings.

name: RODDY DOYLE
description:
Event 91
Roddy Doyle

Roddy Doyle is one of Ireland’s finest and most beloved writers.

From his breakthrough novel The Commitments, later adapted into a major film and his wider Barrytown Trilogy The Snapper and The Van to The Woman Who Walked Into Doors, Smile, Love and his Booker Prize-winning Paddy Clarke Ha Ha Ha, his work has captured the rhythms and voices of working class Dublin and beyond in fiction, short stories and screenplays.

In 2025, he moved from Booker Prize alumni to Chair of the Booker Prize Judges, reading more than 150 books alongside Ayòbámi Adébáyò, Sarah Jessica Parker, Chris Power and Kiley Reid to find the best work of literary fiction of the year.

In this special event, the exceptionally talented and ever-entertaining Doyle sits down with Michèle A’Court to discuss his impressive literary career, his unique year immersed in the books of others, and what he’s working on now that his reading load has lightened and he’s back at the writing desk.

name: THE SPICE SALON
description:
Event 92
The Spice Salon

Back by very popular demand – and better and smuttier than before – The Spice Salon returns to quench Tāmaki’s thirst.

Four stellar Festival authors join host Samuel Te Kani (Ngāpuhi) for late-night readings and discussions of the steamiest bits of their work – of the high-brow, literary variety, of course ...

For your listening pleasure are: Tony Tulathimutte (Rejection), Maria Reva (Endling), Laura Borrowdale (Dead Ends) and Joseph Trinidad (Lucky Creatures).

This was a sell-out event in 2025 and a Festival highlight – so book early.

It’ll banish all memory of English class being (ahem) dry.

name: DROP-IN DRAWING: LITERARY STILL LIFE
description:
Event 93
Drop-in Drawing: Literary Still Life

Start your Festival day the creative way, and have a go at drawing a literary-inspired still life with guidance from art tutor Summer Knight. All skill levels welcome, and materials are provided.

name: INDUSTRY INSIGHTS WITH THE BIG IDEA: THE WHAT’S NEXT? EDITION
description:
Event 94
Industry Insights with The Big Idea: The What’s Next? Edition

Bite-sized interviews between The Big Idea Editor Gabi Lardies and practitioners and professionals in the NZ literary sector. Today she sits down with book-selling insiders to discuss what’s next for a book once it’s published.

name: WORLDWIDE BOOK CLUB: SCOTTISH LITERATURE
description:
Event 95
Worldwide Book Club: Scottish Literature

Edinburgh’s Makar (Poet Laureate) and author of Muckle Flugga, Michael Pedersen is here to introduce you to his favourite Scottish writers from the mainland to across the Isles.

name: DEBUT OF THE DAY: SHANA CHANDRA
description:
Event 96
Debut of the Day: Shana Chandra

Shana Chandra will introduce her literary debut Banjara, a searing story of two women’s reckoning of Indo-Fijian, Girmitiya history, told over 100 years apart.

name: INK BOOK CLUB WITH MANAWAKA AO: HEART LAMP
description:
Event 97
INK Book Club with Manawaka Ao: Heart Lamp

The translator of Heart Lamp by Bhanu Mushtaq, Deepa Bhasthi joins Ara Alam-Simmons as a very special guest to Manawaka Ao’s INK book club. Come kōrero regardless of if you’ve read the book, or are curious to pick it up.

name: BRETON DUKES: WRITING FACT INTO FICTION
description:
Event 98
Breton Dukes: Writing Fact Into Fiction

On a family visit to Bokarina, Queensland, acclaimed fiction writer Breton Dukes (Party Boy, What Sort of Man) found the seed of a short story.

But how do you make the leap from lived experience into exaggeration, invention and actual storytelling?

In this hands-on session, Dukes will take you through his process of writing the short story “Bokarina”, revealing how he navigated the crucial decisions: what to keep, what to discard, what to heighten, and where truth ends and fiction begins.

Participants are encouraged to bring a story idea of their own based on real events and have a go at making some transformations of their own.

Perfect for writers at any stage who want to turn their own experiences into compelling fiction.

name: LILY KING: HEART THE LOVER
description:
Event 99
Lily King: Heart the Lover

Every so often, a novel comes along that critics line up to praise, booksellers hand-sell with ease, and readers breathlessly pass on to other readers in their lives.

Lily King’s Heart the Lover is that novel.

Tracing her protagonist from her 1980s American college years to her midlife reckoning prompted by a surprise visit from her past, King crafts the perfect three act-tale of the lifelong echoes of first love, lives lived out of alignment, and the joys, sorrow and regret of paths not taken.

New to Lily’s work? Fear not. If you like Ann Patchett, David Nicholls, Elizabeth Strout or Sally Rooney, you might just be about to discover your new favourite writer.

Jenna Todd (Kāi Tahu) meets her in her first visit to AWF.

name: DAVID MOSCROP: CANADA AT A CROSSROADS
description:
Event 100
David Moscrop: Canada at a Crossroads
