David Moscrop is one of Canada’s leading political commentators and the author of On Nationalism and Too Dumb for Democracy? Why We Make Bad Political Decisions And How We Can Make Better Ones, inspired by his political science PhD on the psychology of political decision-making.

He is also a contributor, alongside Margaret Atwood and Omar El Akkad, to Elbows Up! – a blazing, urgent anthology of Canadian unity and defiance written in response to President Trump’s threat to make Canada “the 51st state”.

Max Harris meets him in his first visit to AWF to discuss his acclaimed work, incisive commentary and what it means to report from the front lines of political life at a pivotal moment in his country’s history.

name: INDIGENOUS LANGUAGES, TRANSLATION AND MEANING
description:
Event 101
Indigenous Languages, Translation and Meaning

He ao anō kei roto i ngā reo. E matapaki ana ngā kaituhi me ngā mātanga pūmātauranga nō ngā whenua taketake i te reo hei pūnaha e ora ana, e aro pū nei ki te whakamāori, ki te tikanga, me te haepapa.

He aha ngā mea ka ngaro, ka tiakina, ka panonihia rānei i te whakawhitinga ngā mātauranga taketake i tētahi reo ki tētahi?

Ka matapaki a Hēmi Kelly (nō Ngāti Maniapoto, nō Ngāti Tahu, nō Ngāti Whaoa hoki) nō Aotearoa, rātou ko Louise Erdrich (he mema nō te Turtle Mountain Band o Chippewa) nō Amerika, ko David A. Robertson (he mema nō te Norway House Cree Nation) nō Kānata, i ngā tāraitanga, i te manaakitanga, me te ātetetanga e hāngai ana ki te whakapūmautanga, ki te whakarauoratanga rānei o tētahi reo taketake, me te huritao ki te wāhi ki te reo i a koe e whai ana ki te tū hei tipuna pai ki ngā uri whakaheke.

He Wāhanga nō tā Shilo Kino Noho hei Kairauhī Manuhiri.

Indigenous languages carry worlds within them. Writers and scholars from across Indigenous nations join to discuss language as a living system, with a particular focus on translation, meaning and responsibility.

What is lost, protected or transformed when Indigenous knowledge moves between languages?

Aotearoa New Zealand’s Hēmi Kelly (Ngāti Maniapoto, Ngāti Tahu, Ngāti Whaoa), America’s Louise Erdrich (a member of the Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa) and Canada’s David A. Robertson (member of Norway House Cree Nation) discuss the craft, care and resistance of sustaining or reviving an Indigenous language and reflect on what role language plays in being a good ancestor to those that follow us.

Part of Shilo Kino’s Guest Curatorship

This session will be held in English.

name: POLKINGHORNE: INSIDE THE TRIAL OF THE CENTURY
description:
Event 102
Polkinghorne: Inside the Trial of the Century

In his Ockham NZ Book Awards longlisted book Polkinghorne Steve Braunias takes readers on a revealing and chilling journey through one of the most high-profile murder cases in recent Aotearoa New Zealand history.

The death of Pauline Hanna in her home in Remuera, and the arrest of her eye surgeon husband, Dr Philip Polkinghorne, led to an epic trial that played out like a scandalous exposé of rich Auckland life.

Braunias joins Lady Deborah Chambers KC to discuss his unique insight into the investigation and trial, the ethics of researching and writing true crime, and with the coronial inquest scheduled for soon after the Festival, his predictions for any further twists in the dark tale.

name: BETSY BALLOON
description:
Event 103
Betsy Balloon

Welcome to the island of Helium where Betsy lives with her balloon factory-owning family.

They’re cheerful, a bit money-mad and life is on the up. But when the world turns against balloons, their world pops and Betsy must start a new life in a faraway city with her eccentric uncle, Hercules Snark, an inventor of mostly half-finished things.

Unfortunately, Snark has problems of his own. In his city, it never stops raining. The streets are flooding. And things – quite literally – are starting to sink.

Betsy Balloon is a rambunctious modern fable presented as a staged reading with original music and illustration based on a story by this year’s Honoured Writer, Bill Manhire.

Best suited for ages 5-10 years.

name: THAMMIKA SONGKAEO: WRITING FEAR, ANGER, AND SADNESS
description:
Event 104
Thammika Songkaeo: Writing Fear, Anger, and Sadness

Join novelist, non-fiction writer and film producer Thammika Songkaeo (Stamford Hospital) for a masterclass on crafting high-impact emotion without melodrama.

This session will break down how difficult emotions reach readers – not through naming feelings, but through precision, restraint and scene design. You will explore how fear operates through pacing and uncertainty, how anger reshapes voice and action, and how sadness emerges through omission, rhythm and detail.

Using targeted writing exercises, you’ll learn how to translate internal states into action, sensation and subtext, and how to revise based on emotional effect rather than intention. Designed for fiction and narrative non-fiction writers alike, the masterclass will equip you with tools to apply immediately to scenes of fear, anger and sadness in your work.

name: DAV PILKEY: SUPERCHARGED CREATIVITY!
description:
Event 105
Dav Pilkey: Supercharged Creativity!

Get ready for a special event featuring renowned author and illustrator Dav Pilkey! The #1 global best-selling creator of Dog Man, Captain Underpants and Cat Kid Comic Club who has fired up the imaginations of millions of kids around the world, started creating comics as a young kid when he was made to sit out in the hallway for being disruptive in class.

Fast-forward to today and millions of copies of his books have been sold worldwide and even turned into films, musicals and TV series. Jump inside the worlds of your favourite Dav Pilkey book series in this fun-filled session featuring a visual presentation and live drawing by Dav Pilkey, plus trivia, special prizes, giveaways and more!

The event will be followed by a photo opportunity with Dav Pilkey. Attendees are encouraged to power up and dress up as Captain Underpants, Dog Man, Cat Kid, or your favourite Dav Pilkey book and join our Character Parade: details on page 54.

name: WRITING THE CLIMATE RECKONING
description:
Event 106
Writing the Climate Reckoning

The country began the year in mourning after extreme weather resulted in fatal landslips at Mount Maunganui and Welcome Bay.

Meanwhile, deadly winter storms in the US claimed numerous lives and disrupted thousands more and a global analysis revealed that 50 of the world’s 100 biggest cities now face severe water stress.

It’s a frightening time – and those were just January’s headlines.

Sarosh Mulla meets three writers whose work, across fiction and non-fiction, confront the climate emergency head-on: esteemed Indian novelist and thinker Amitav Ghosh, eco-thriller author Charlotte McConaghy (Wild Dark Shore, Migrations) and Toha Network co-founder David Hall (Kiwis in Climate).

They discuss their most pressing concerns about what lies ahead of us – and the action they hope their writing might spark.

name: TAREQ BACONI
description:
Event 107
Tareq Baconi

“I am forever changed after reading this book”, “outstanding” and “achingly beautiful” are just some of how fellow authors have described renowned Palestinian scholar Tareq Baconi’s moving memoir, Fire in Every Direction.

At once a love story, a coming-of-age tale and a diasporic narrative, it takes us from the Middle East to London, and from 1948 to the present, as Baconi traces generations of his family’s displacement through war, as well as his own political and queer awakening in the face of other forms of exile and oppression.

What emerges is a powerful examination of the meaning of home, impossible love amidst unimaginable odds, and a fierce fight for a clear sense of self against the most fractured of backdrops.

Kaan Hiini (Te Arawa, Ngāpuhi, Te Rarawa, Pākehā) meets him.

name: A HEAVY HISTORY: GIRMITIYAS IN FIJI
description:
Event 108
A Heavy History: Girmitiyas in Fiji

In May 1879, a ship with 463 Indian indentured labourers docked in Fiji.

Over the next 37 years, more than 60,500 workers – who came to be known as the Girmitiyas – were sent from India to work in Fiji’s sugarcane and tobacco plantations under inhumane conditions.

Banjara, the literary debut by Shana Chandra, is two women’s reckoning of their Indo–Fijian history, told over 100 years apart.

Krish Naidu is the president of the Fiji Girmit Foundation New Zealand, founded to honour the journey, struggles and achievements of Fiji Indians from their Girmit roots to life in contemporary Aotearoa.

Kiran Dass joins them in the same week as Girmit Remembrance Day to discuss this dark period of Pacific history not widely known.

name: CARDS ON THE TABLE: TAROT AND WRITING
description:
Event 109
Cards on the Table: Tarot and Writing

Tarot is used to predict the future, but what if it could also unlock creativity?

Claire Mabey (The Raven’s Eye Rebellion) presents a practical guide to using tarot as a creative tool, exploring archetypal characters and how the cards can help break through artistic block.

No tarot experience is required, but if you have a deck of your own, feel free to bring it along.

name: THE RISE OF ROMANCE
description:
Event 110
The Rise of Romance

From the dragon- and fae-filled worlds of romantasy, to the steamy locker rooms of Heated Rivalry-style sport romances, or the soft glow of more gentle styles, romance is one of the hottest genres in the book world right now.

Ruby Wallace, Ivy Cliffwater and Shanna Tan join Emma Wehipeihana (Ngāti Tukorehe, Ngāti Porou) to spill on why it has readers hooked and where it’s headed next.

Supported by Korean Cultural Centre.

name: DREAMING FOR THE OCEAN IN US: TANGATA MOANA WRITERS AND DESIRE
description:
Event 111
Dreaming for the Ocean In Us: Tangata Moana writers and Desire

A bold, intimate conversation exploring Pacific writing beyond expectation and what writers are reaching for when they choose desire as a compass. Speaking from different career stages, they explore notions of longing, ambition, pleasure and vulnerability, and how these forces move through their creative practice. Featuring Tusiata Avia, Amber Esau and Danielle Kionasina Dilys Thomson, with Ruby Macomber.

name: NO ONE CARES ABOUT YOUR WRITING THE WAY YOU DO
description:
Event 112
No One Cares About Your Writing the Way You Do

Bestselling author Dominic Hoey (1985) shares practical tools for thriving outside the traditional system and industry.

Covering the ups and downs of the DIY path, he’ll share the tools writers need, the importance of building community and when to outsource versus staying self-reliant.

Expect tips on funding, publishing and growing an audience - and ultimately, how to grow the confidence to forge a creative path.

name: TO HELL AND BACK
description:
Event 113
To Hell and Back

Travel down to the depths of hell with three dynamic writers who uncover the underworld in their vivid reimaginings.

RF Kuang’s Katabasis sends Cambridge rivals on a deadly academic descent; Rachel Smythe reshapes Hades and Persephone in Lore Olympus; Nikita Gill’s Hekate charts a goddess’ rise from child of war to all-powerful witch.

Claire Mabey joins the trio to delve into their myth‑soaked tales.

Supported by Culture Ireland.

name: THINKING DIFFERENTLY
description:
Event 114
Thinking Differently

We’re not all wired the same.

Madeleine Chapman meets Josh Silver (Traumaland, Fruit Fly), David A. Robertson (All the Little Monsters) and Karina McHardy (All In: A Mother’s Journey Through Autism) to discuss navigating neurodiversity and mental health, and the fight for better support.

Supported by the British Council New Zealand and the Pacific.

name: QUEER THE PAGE
description:
Event 115
Queer the Page

Explore new ways of seeing words by reclaiming and queering texts in a Blackout Poetry workshop and kōrero with Hebe Kearney and Nathan Joe.

Learn about their work as queer poets, the history and practice of blackout poetry and its power to uplift minority voices.

Create your own poem by redacting and highlighting words from old books using markers and fabric.

name: WEIRD GIRL LIT
description:
Event 116
Weird Girl Lit

Cosy’s out. Weird's in.

Jen Eastwood of Sick Sad Lit dives into the deliciously strange with Mieko Kawakami (Sisters in Yellow, Breasts and Eggs), Bora Chung (The Midnight Timetable, Cursed Bunny) and Laura Borrowdale (Dead Ends and Sex, with Animals) to chat about using the darkly imaginative, the speculative or the surreal to capture the modern realities of life for women.

Supported by Literature Translation Institute of Korea.

name: HOT TAKES, SHARP LINES: A SPOKEN WORD ROUND TABLE
description:
Event 117
Hot Takes, Sharp Lines: A Spoken Word Round Table

Compelling novelist and poet Dominic Hoey (1985) hosts a Spoken Word Round Table pulsing with dynamic poetry, sharp wit and unforgettable storytelling. Featuring poet baddies Mr Meaty Boy (Ngāti Porou, Ngāpuhi), Liam Jacobson (Kāi Tahu), Amber Esau (Ngāpuhi, Manase) and Edinburgh Makar (Poet Laureate) Michael Pedersen.

Expect lyrical finesse, surprising turns, mischief and performances that linger long after the last line is spoken.

Supported by Scottish Books International.

name: DYSTOPIA AS PROTEST: WRITE THE FUTURE THAT FIGHTS BACK
description:
Event 118
Dystopia as Protest: Write the Future that Fights Back

Discover how dystopian fiction questions the present and disrupts the status quo.

Join author Josh Silver (HappyHead, Traumaland) to explore how writers use imagined worlds to examine power, control and the stories societies tell themselves. You’ll examine how real‑world issues become the foundations of dystopian fiction and learn how to create your own speculative worlds rooted in contemporary concerns.

Supported by the British Council New Zealand and the Pacific.

name: LOVE WON, LOVE LOST, LOVE BURNED UP IN FLAMES
description:
Event 119
Love Won, Love Lost, Love Burned Up in Flames

Hearts soaring, hearts broken and everything in between. Seven writers tell intimate, passionate, uplifting, devastating stories of love.

Expect beautiful starbursts of tears, laughter, breathtaking honesty and openness, each told in seven-minute moments at the mic.

Curated and hosted by Michael and Matariki Bennett (Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Whakaue).

name: 95BFM’S THE WIRE: QUESTION TIME
description:
Event 120
95bFM’s The Wire: Question Time

Castor Chacko, host of bFM’s The Wire, brings together Max Harris and Gabriella Brayne (Ngāti Maniapoto, Falefa, Pākehā) to break down the week’s biggest stories in Aotearoa and explore the issues that matter to you.

The panel goes behind the headlines, from Te Tiriti and climate change to health, transport and the cost of living, to deliver debate and insights that matter to the lives of those in Tāmaki Makaurau and nationwide.

name: VICTOR RODGER: PUTTING YOUR RAGE ON THE PAGE
description:
Event 121
Victor Rodger: Putting Your Rage on the Page

“Write what you know,” they say. But bringing real lives, and all they contain, to the page or stage can be a fraught business.

Among its numerous prizes and acclaim, Victor Rodger’s Sons, a semi-autobiographical play about absent fathers, also brought with it hard lessons about speaking your truth – and that of others.

Join the award-winning playwright, producer and IMML creative writing tutor for a session that explores the complexities of putting emotionally charged work inspired by your life, or the lives of those around you, into the world.

He’ll reflect on his lessons from Sons, discuss some examples from the wider industry, and lead you on some exercises and prompts to help you shape your own projects.

name: FRONTLINES AND HEADLINES: THE LIFE OF A FOREIGN CORRESPONDENT
description:
Event 122
Frontlines and Headlines: The Life of a Foreign Correspondent

They’re the faces, voices and wordsmiths who bring us frontline news from around the world.

But what are the realities of life as a foreign correspondent? Its highs, lows, challenges, joys? What drives someone towards a career that involves so much danger, personal sacrifice and often years away from home?

Lifting the lid on one of the most visible yet scarcely understood roles in journalism are the BBC’s Chief International Correspondent Lyse Doucet (The Finest Hotel in Kabul: A People’s History of Afghanistan); Barbara Dreaver (Be Brave) who has covered the Pacific for over 30 years and Charlotte Glennie (Every Second Counts), TVNZ’s first Asia correspondent who has spent over two decades telling our region’s stories for newsrooms and not-for-profits.

Chaired by Susie Ferguson.

name: ELIZABETH KNOX: NIGHT, MA
description:
Event 123
Elizabeth Knox: Night, Ma

For three and a half years, calamities hit Elizabeth Knox’s life in rapid succession.

Her sister suffered a psychotic break and was hospitalised against her will, her husband’s brother died by violence, and her mother was diagnosed with motor neurone disease.

A beloved storyteller and author of more than 14 novels including The Vintner’s Luck and The Absolute Book, Knox has given us many worlds. In her landmark memoir Night, Ma, she invites us into her own.

The result is a generous and transcendent reflection on time, illness and loss; about the daily work love and care require; and the net of family that people are held by, but also slip through.

She joins Emily Perkins to discuss it.

name: HANNAH KENT: A LOVE LETTER TO ICELAND
description:
Event 124
Hannah Kent: A Love Letter to Iceland

Many readers will know leading Australian author Hannah Kent from Burial Rites, her haunting historical novel about a young woman accused of murder in 19th-century Iceland.

Yet the novel’s trajectory – and indeed Hannah’s path to becoming one of Australia’s leading literary novelists – all stemmed from a chance exchange year she took in Iceland at the age of 17, an experience that would indelibly shape her as a writer and a person.

In her luminous memoir Always Home, Always Homesick she revisits this transformative year, tracks her ever-evolving relationship with Iceland, and reflects on the pleasures and pains of navigating a life and identity split between two very different places.

Chaired by Kim Hill.

name: THE WAITANGI TRIBUNAL - 50 YEARS
description:
Event 125
The Waitangi Tribunal - 50 Years

Late 2025 marked 50 years since the establishment of the Waitangi Tribunal, an institution that has had a pivotal role in Aotearoa New Zealand’s history and identity.

50 Years of The Waitangi Tribunal – WhakamanaiTeTiriti collates essays from a range of contributors surveying the decisions, results and reports of the Tribunal over its half a century.

Spanning land, water, the settlements process, the Kaupapa Inquiries, social policy, mana and rangatiratanga,it represents the breadth of issues considered by the Tribunal, and its impact on complex legal concepts, representation of communities and understanding of TeTiriti o Waitangi.

The book’s co-editor Carwyn Jones (Ngāti Kahungunu, Te Aitanga-a-Māhaki) and contributors Margaret Mutu (Ngāti Kahu, Te Rarawa, Ngāti Whātua) and Jacqueline Paul (Ngāpuhi, Ngāti Tūwharetoa, Ngāti Kahungunu ki Heretaunga) join Dale Husband (Ngāti Maru) to reflect on the Tribunal’s anniversary, and what its future might hold.

name: ZINES NZ: FROM PUNK TO PRESENT
description:
Event 126
Zines NZ: from Punk to Present

Lo-fi, hand-built, fiercely independent and often with a healthy dose of humour and anarchy, zines are a fascinating form of publishing.

The history of them in Aotearoa New Zealand is equally so.

Zine expert (and the creator of the country’s longest-running zine, Incredibly Hot Sex with Hideous People) Bryce Galloway joins Jo Randerson to dive into the characters, movements and zines that comprise the form’s compelling story in this part of the world.

From the first punk-rock music zine in 1980, to the boom of zinefests in the early 21st century, Galloway will show some landmark examples and discuss why this handmade form has endured through generational, societal and technological shifts.

name: LOUISE ERDRICH
description:
Event 127
Louise Erdrich

From her National Book Critics Circle Award-winning debut novel, Love Medicine, her National Book Award-winning The Round House to her Pulitzer Prize-winning The Night Watchman, Louise Erdrich is one of American literature’s most celebrated and highly regarded writers.

Born to an Ojibwe mother and a German father, Erdrich’s works have brought the Native American experience, and those of mixed heritage in America to the centre of cultural and literary discourse, and earned her admiration from readers and critics alike.

Paula Morris meets the esteemed writer The Boston Globe called “one of the world’s literary giants” in her much-anticipated first AWF appearance to reflect on an exceptional career and discuss her latest novel, The Mighty Red and short story collection, The Python’s Kiss.

name: THE MUSHROOM TAPES
description:
Event 128
The Mushroom Tapes

In The Mushroom Tapes, Helen Garner, Chloe Hooper and Sarah Krasnostein

- three renowned writers of true crime - turn their attentions to the trial of Erin Patterson, the woman dubbed “the mushroom lady” after three guests died from death-cap poisoning at a family lunch.

Told through their intimate conversations – phone calls, car rides and the strange rhythms of court days – their account draws the readers close to the case’s surreal details, and balances scepticism and fascination to document and dissect the riveting case.

Helen Garner joins Kim Hill to unpack our collective obsession with this “strange and terrible crime” in a small Australian town that made headlines across the world.

name: NO COUNTRY FOR MIDDLE-AGED MEN?
description:
Event 129
No Country for Middle-Aged Men?

When David Szalay’s spare, quietly devastating novel Flesh won the 2025 Booker Prize, it prompted conversations about modern masculinity, the long echo of unresolved trauma and the consequences of a life shaped more by circumstance than agency.

In Breton Duke’s Party Boy, a harried husband and father approaches his 50th birthday and finds that the strategies he’s long used to numb or outrun the events of his youth are failing him.

Scottish writer Michael Pedersen’s memoir Boy Friends is a tender, life-affirming tribute to a friend lost to suicide, and a manifesto for the sustaining power of male friendship and honest communication.

They join Adam Dudding to discuss their books, and the vital questions they raise about what it means to be a man today.

name: BARRY SOPER: ONE LAST QUESTION, PRIME MINISTER
description:
Event 130
Barry Soper: One Last Question, Prime Minister

Spanning Muldoon to Luxon, Barry Soper has spent the best part of five decades reporting from New Zealand’s corridors of power.

In One Last Question, Prime Minister, he traces covering the 12 prime ministers of his working life and reveals the inside scoop from the halls, hotels and side-rooms where the country’s chosen leaders – for better or worse – created their lasting legacy.

“It’s fair to say Barry knew, and still knows, where all the bones are buried,” said former prime minister Sir John Key.

He sits down with Danyl McLauchlan to look back on a career spent reporting from the heart of the nation’s political life.

name: BRYAN WALPERT: DIGGING SIDEWAYS INTO CHARACTER
description:
Event 131
Bryan Walpert: Digging Sideways into Character

People often talk about deepening character.

In this masterclass Bryan Walpert, author of the Ockham NZ Book Awards longlisted novel Empathy, will describe how to go deep by digging sideways into character – doing so organically so that you discover who your character is as you write, surprising even yourself.

Walpert, whose work has been noted for “the rigorous internality of each character”, will offer a technique founded on the principle that as the writer you are in complete control of time.

You’ll practise moving in a single passage from a snippet of dialogue or seemingly minor detail into an empathetic understanding of what motivates your character - and move your character from idea to flesh and blood.

name: LYSE DOUCET
description:
Event 132
Lyse Doucet

In her distinguished career – latterly as the BBC’s Chief International Correspondent – Lyse Doucet has brought news from the world’s conflict zones, and the human stories within them, to millions.

In The Finest Hotel in Kabulshe uses the story of Afghanistan’s first luxury hotel – which opened in 1969 and has endured Soviet occupation, multiple coups, a grievous civil war, a US invasion and the rise, fall and rise of the Taliban in its 50 years – to craft a richly immersive history of modern Afghanistan.

In her first appearance at AWF, she joins Paula Penfold to reflects on an extraordinary life in the world’s geopolitical hotspots, and the remarkable Kabul hotel that helped her tell the story of a nation and its people.

Supported by Platinum Patron Dame Jenny Gibbs.

name: SLIDING DOORS
description:
Event 133
Sliding Doors

We all have them.

Points in our lives where a seemingly small moment, event or decision forked our path and led our life down a whole other route.

Where – or indeed, who – would you be if things had gone even slightly differently?

What different threads would have made up the tapestry of your life as you know it?

Petra Bagust meets three writers whose compelling and best-selling novels explore this in fascinating ways: Lily King (Heart the Lover), Florence Knapp (The Names) and Josie Shapiro (Good Things Come and Go).

Together they discuss the small moments in life that become the big, and the allure of imagining the paths not taken.

name: KO AU TE WHENUA, KO TE WHENUA KO AU I AM THE LAND, AND THE LAND IS ME.
description:
Event 134
Ko au te whenua, ko te whenua ko au I am the land, and the land is me.

He aha te pānga i waenga i te motukina o tō tātou hononga ki ngā whenua, ki tetaiao, ki ngā pūnaha kai hoki, me ngā tairaru o te wā? He aha ngā pānga i waenga i te tūhonohono anō me ngā ara ki te whakaoranga anō, ki te tauritenga, ki te manaaki hoki i ngā reanga kei mua i te aroaro?

Ka hōpara te ringa whāomoomo, a Tame Malcolm (nō Te Arawa, nō Ngāti Ruanui hoki) rātou ko te kaiwhakaako i te maramataka, ko Rikki Solomon (nō Ngati Kahungunu, nō Ngati Kahu ki Whangaroa hoki), ko te kaituhi hoki o Pātaka Kai: Growing Kai Sovereignty, ko Dr Jessica Hutchings (nō Ngāi Tahu, nō Ngāti Huirapa, he Gujarati hoki) i tō tātou hononga ki te taiao me ngā pānga ki tōna whakatōhenehenetanga.

Mā roto mai i te whakanōhanga o te mātauranga Māori hei pūnaha mātauranga e ora ana, ka kōtuitui tēnei kōrero, e arahina nei e Mandy Kupenga (nō Ngāti Porou, nō Te Aitanga a Mate hoki), i te maramataka, ite rangatiratanga o te kai, me te hauora hei mātai i te oranga o te taiao, i ngā kare ā-roto, me te porihanga i ēnei rā nei.

Me pēhea tātou e mōhio ai ka ora tonu ngā whenua mō ngā reanga e whai ake nei i a tātou?

He Wāhanga nō tā Shilo Kino Noho hei Kairauhī Manuhiri.

How does disconnection from whenua, te taiao and food systems shape contemporary crises? How might reconnection offer pathways towards healing, balance and care for future generations?

Conservationist Tame Malcolm (Te Arawa, Ngāti Ruanui), maramataka practitioner Rikki Solomon (Ngati Kahungunu, Ngati Kahu ki Whangaroa) and author of Pātaka Kai: Growing Kai Sovereignty Dr Jessica Hutchings (Ngāi Tahu, Ngāti Huirapa, Gujarati) explore our relationship with te taiao and the impacts of its disruption.

Centring mātauranga Māori as a living knowledge system, this kōrero guided by Mandy Kupenga (Ngāti Porou, Te Aitanga a Mate) weaves maramataka, kai sovereignty and hauora to examine environmental, emotional and social well-being today.

How can we ensure the land’s well-being for the generations that follow us?

Part of Shilo Kino’s Guest Curatorship.

This session will be held in English.

name: SECRET ART POWERS: ACHIEVING CHANGE THROUGH CREATIVE THINKING
description:
Event 135
Secret Art Powers: Achieving Change Through Creative Thinking

Does your career need a pivot? Talk to a dancer ...

Are resources scarce? Ask painters how they make something from nothing ...

In this dynamic theatrical lecture, celebrated artist Jo Randerson (whom Flight of the Conchords’ Bret McKenzie called “one of New Zealand’s greatest creative minds”) explores the secret powers artists use daily and shares six creative mindsets that can help us navigate our complex and changing times.

Joyful, generous, affirming, nourishing and timely, it’s the perfect way to harness all the creativity you’ve experienced at the Festival, and think about how to employ lessons from artists to create positive, lasting change in your life or work.

name: THE 2025 BOOKER PRIZE WINNER: DAVID SZALAY
description:
Event 136
The 2025 Booker Prize Winner: David Szalay

When the Booker Prize judges met to discuss the six stellar books shortlisted for the 2025 prize, there was one book to which they kept returning in their five-hour deliberations: Hungarian–British author David Szalay’s Flesh.

A spare and propulsive novel, it follows a man from adolescence to old age as he is unravelled by a series of events beyond his grasp, and asks profound questions about what makes a life worth living – and what breaks it.

Roddy Doyle, Chair of judges, remarked “[w]e had never read anything quite like it” and described it as an “extraordinary, singular novel”.

In this special event, Doyle talks to Szalay about his lauded book and hears his reflections six months on from winning the life-changing prize.

Supported by Platinum Patrons Betsy & Michael Benjamin.

name: LESSONS ON LIVING: A TRIBUTE TO NIGEL LATTA
description:
Event 137
Lessons on Living: A Tribute to Nigel Latta

When he was diagnosed with terminal cancer and told he only had a few months to live, psychologist Nigel Latta – known for his three-decade career teaching resilience and navigating life’s ups and downs – had his own toolkit put to the ultimate test.

In his last book, Lessons on Living, Latta condenses a career worth of advice, guidance and life experience into simple principles for all that quickly became one of the year’s biggest-selling NZ books.

His widow Dr Natalie Flynn, grief expert Dr Lucy Hone and friend to whom Latta gave one of his final interviews, Guyon Espiner, come together to pay tribute and reflect on Latta’s remarkable life, contribution and the legacy he leaves behind.

name: THE DEAD SPEAK: A LIFE IN FORENSICS
description:
Event 138
The Dead Speak: A Life in Forensics

Between them they have 60 years of experience working in crime scenes and disaster zones across Auckland, the country, and the world, including the city’s darkest corners, the Christchurch earthquake and the Boxing Day tsunami in Thailand.

Don’t miss this rare chance to go beyond the police tape and hear about the world of forensics from two of our most seasoned investigators: Thomas Coyle (The Dead Speak) and Ruben Miller (The Blood Says Otherwise).

With Steve Braunias (Polkinghorne) they’ll discuss the inner-workings of their profession, the most impactful cases and scenes they’ve worked on, and the personal toll of witnessing humanity’s darkest sides and moments in their daily work.

name: FESTIVAL WRAP-UP
description:
Event 139
Festival Wrap-Up

There’s something very special about the “bubble” that forms at Festivals like AWF.

For a few days you’ve stepped out of your regular life and routines and immersed yourself in a world of books, writers and all the unexpected joys, connections and surprises that festivals bring.

We hear time and time again that festivalgoers don’t quite know where to put that energy at the end of it all ...

So, before the bookish bubble bursts, and life and its responsibilities rudely intrude, come revel with other festivalgoers and share your favourite moments, your most surprising discoveries, or most impactful encounters of AWF26 – or just listen to those shared by our panel hosted by Bookety Book Books’ Mandy Myles, before we move into the final event of the Festival.

name: RT HON DAME JACINDA ARDERN: A DIFFERENT KIND OF POWER
description:
Event 140
Rt Hon Dame Jacinda Ardern: A Different Kind of Power

Former prime minister Jacinda Ardern was Aotearoa New Zealand’s youngest female leader, and her Ockham NZ Book Awards shortlisted memoir, A Different Kind of Power, has been the biggest-selling locally published book by a fair margin, and became an international bestseller with its advocacy for strong, empathy-driven leadership.

From a small rural town childhood with a police officer father and a working mother, to becoming her country’s youngest prime minister in more than 150 years and only the second world leader to give birth while in office – it spans both an extraordinary personal journey and an unprecedented period in our country’s history: from the 2019 Christchurch mosque attacks to a global pandemic.

Threaded through both its personal and political strands is Ardern’s unwavering belief in kindness and compassion – and a powerful call for these to be recognised as strengths, not weaknesses, in modern leadership.

She sits down with Noelle McCarthy to discuss her notable path and reflect on the lessons she hopes can empower us all to create a kinder, more hopeful future.

name: THE FARMER’S PYJAMA PARTY!
description:
Event 143
The Farmer’s Pyjama Party!

Lions, dinosaurs, jellyfish and kiwi – this is not your average farm!

Get curious in this sparkling interactive read-aloud by Ruth Paul  as she brings a handful of her charming picture books off the page and onto the stage using props and sound. You’ll even get to giggle along to her brand-new picture book The Farmer’s Pyjamas.

And yes – you’re welcome to wear your pyjamas!

Illustration from The Farmer’s Pyjamas © 2026 Ruth Paul. Reproduced by permission of Walker Books Australia Pty Ltd. All rights reserved.

name: NIU TUPU TALES
description:
Event 144
Niu Tupu Tales

Get whisked away on a joyful adventure to the island of Niue and discover the wonders of niu (coconuts).

Inangaro Vakaafi’s playful book Hunt for Niu Tupu celebrates cultural knowledge and discovering treasures in your own backyard.

Gather close as Inangaro shares her story, reveals the many uses of niu and gets you on your feet dancing and singing about the coconut.

name: CREATE WITH NATURE
description:
Event 145
Create with Nature

Inspired by Thank you, Forest, by Finnish author Johanna Venho and illustrator Sanna Pelliccioni, this creative session with Oratia Books teaches you how to craft your own nature notebook celebrating imagination and the beauty of the natural world.

Thank you, Forest, by Johanna Venho, illustrated by Sanna Pelliccioni, translated by Jen Pulju Porter. Published by Five Oceans, an imprint of Oratia Books, 2025.

name: FIGS AND THEIR FANS
description:
Event 146
Figs and their fans

Champi loves juicy, fresh figs but she’s not the only one! Follow Champi and her brother on a fun-filled adventure to meet playful animals who can’t resist this tasty fruit. International Booker Prize winner Deepa Bhasthi reads Champi and the Fig Tree in English and Kannada, and shares tales from her own farm in India. A warm, engaging event for curious kids eager to learn more about animals and nature.

Supported by SALT (South Asian Literature in Translation)

ಅಂಜೂರಅಂಜೂರಯಾರಅಂಜೂರ/ Anjoora, Anjoora, Yaara Anjoora, written by Deepa Bhasthi, illustrated by Joanna Mendes, published by Pratham Books, 2024.

name: JUNIOR SLEUTHS WANTED!
description:
Event 147
Junior Sleuths Wanted!

Help our hound crack the case!

Join Detective Stanley for a mystery-solving adventure that will put your sleuthing skills to the test. Work alongside creators of the Detective Stanley series, Hannah Tunnicliffe and Erica Harrison, to uncover clues, assess the suspects and pronounce: “Case Closed!”

Junior Detectives will leave with instructions for drawing Stanley and creating their very own comic-strip mystery.

Recommended for 5+ but all ages welcome.

Illustrations©Erica Harrison 2026

name: THE BOOK DOCTOR IS IN!
description:
Event 148
The Book Doctor Is In!

Got a serious case of “what-do-I-read-next-itis?” or “post-dream-book-blues?” Then it’s time to visit The Book Doctor!

Line up for your very own book prescription from the team at The National Library. Tell them your favourite reads and they’ll diagnose the perfect cure for your next reading adventure!

name: BEASTLY MAGIC
description:
Event 149
Beastly Magic

Little witches and wizards, it’s time to summon a beast!

Come plot and scheme with our Grand High Witch, Claire Mabey (The Raven’s Eye Rebellion), and design a spell that will conjure a one-of-a-kind creature. Dream up your magical ingredients, toss them into the bubbling cauldron, and together we’ll bring a wondrous beast to life while illustrator Alba Gil Celdrán  (Dragonfall)  captures your creation in a spellbinding illustration.

Recommended for 5+ but all ages welcome.

name: THE BOOK DOCTOR IS IN!
description:
Event 150
The Book Doctor Is In!

Got a serious case of “what-do-I-read-next-itis?” or “post-dream-book-blues?” Then it’s time to visit The Book Doctor!

Line up for your very own book prescription from the team at The National Library. Tell them your favourite reads and they’ll diagnose the perfect cure for your next reading adventure!

name: READY TO ROCK!
description:
Event 151
Ready to Rock!

Meet Violet Grumble, rock star in the making, and her band of ragtag friends. Armed with big band dreams and plenty of mysteries to unravel, join author Rachael King as she takes you on a wild ride through her loveable series Violet and the Velvets.

Expect an electric event of music and storytelling, sure to inspire your own mystery stories. Recommended for 5+ but all ages welcome.

Illustration ©Phoebe Morris

name: WHISKERS & WILD QUESTS
description:
Event 152
Whiskers & Wild Quests

What if your pet became the hero of an epic adventure? Join bestselling author Stacy Gregg  (Ngāti Mahuta, Ngāti Pukeko, Ngāti Maru Hauraki) for a highly creative, animal-filled session inspired by her thrilling book The Last Journey.

Join in the fun, hear about what it takes to bring your own pets alive on the page and even take to the stage to participate in a wild, whiskery performance for cat-lovers with main character kitty energy!

Recommended for 7+ but all ages welcome.

name: KUPU FOUND
description:
Event 153
Kupu Found

“My Mum is collecting words. You see, a long time ago, lots of the words were left out or replaced. Slowly they were forgotten.”

Author Kimberley Ngareta Kearney (Ngāti Maniapoto, Tūhoe) shares her touching pukapuka The Lost Words where a mother reclaims te reo Māori for her family. Afterwards, tamariki and their adults are invited to take part in a playful game, learning everyday kupu they can use together beyond the Festival.

Illustrations © Taylor Terewai Tiave @by.terewai

name: THE FARMER’S PYJAMA PARTY!
description:
Event 154
The Farmer’s Pyjama Party!

Lions, dinosaurs, jellyfish and kiwi – this is not your average farm!

Get curious in this sparkling interactive read-aloud by Ruth Paul  as she brings a handful of her charming picture books off the page and onto the stage using props and sound. You’ll even get to giggle along to her brand-new picture book The Farmer’s Pyjamas.

And yes – you’re welcome to wear your pyjamas!

Illustration from The Farmer’s Pyjamas © 2026 Ruth Paul. Reproduced by permission of Walker Books Australia Pty Ltd. All rights reserved.

name: RIDE ALONG WITH LULUBUG
description:
Event 155
Ride along with Lulubug

Lulubug, Granny P’s beloved 1970s red Morris Minor, is having a few troubles after years of adventures and it might be time for a replacement ride.

Enjoy Paula Walsh’s charming story, New Wheels for Granny P, then help guide Lulubug home in a fun interactive activity.

We’ll also celebrate the launch of Little Bug Books. We'll provide the party hats; you bring the vibe.

Toot toot!

name: FROM PAGE TO PARADE
description:
Event 156
From Page to Parade

Mask making from 10am

Parade from 10.30am

Aotea Square

Celebrate Dav Pilkey’s beloved characters with a parade in Aotea Square! Dress up as your favourite hero or villain or come from 10am to make a mask before the big event.

Please note that if it rains, the parade will be cancelled. Families are warmly invited to head to Level 5 for the Pukapuka fun instead.

name: FEATHERED FINDINGS - BIRD SPOTTING STATIONS
description:
Event 157
Feathered Findings - Bird spotting stations

Bird spotting stations: 10.00am-3.00pm

Giselle’s Tour: 10.30-11.00am & 1.00-1.30pm

Aotea Square

Flock to Aotea Square and become a top bird investigator with the help of Giselle Clarkson’s fact stations. Inspired by her exquisite and comedic book, Omnibird, you’ll discover the extraordinary features of ordinary birds that live above our heads every day.

Explore the stations at your own pace, or join Giselle for a lively, interactive tour packed with tips for budding avian detectives.

Rain or shine, we will be bird spotting.

name: BOOKTOK MEET-CUTE
description:
Event 157
BookTok Meet-Cute

Join our resident BookTokker @CarwenReads on the café side of the Q Theatre Foyer at 12pm for a relaxed BookTok hangout.

Meet fellow book lovers, make some new friends, enjoy cute crafting at the tables, and then head off to the sessions together.

The perfect way to find your bookish people!

name: FEATHERED FINDINGS - GISELLE’S TOUR
description:
Event 158
Feathered Findings - Giselle’s Tour

Bird spotting stations: 10.00am-3.00pm

Giselle’s Tour: 10.30-11.00am & 1.00-1.30pm

Aotea Square

Event 159
Feathered Findings - Giselle’s Tour

Aotea Square
